## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Define the following terms with respect to a flow network:**
> (a) Capacity constraint
> (b) Flow conservation
> (c) Augmenting path

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Capacity constraint:** For every edge $(u, v)$ in the flow network, the assigned flow $f(u, v)$ must not exceed the edge's capacity $c(u, v)$. Formally, $0 \leq f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$ for all $u, v \in V$. This ensures no edge is "overloaded" beyond its physical limit.

(b) **Flow conservation:** For every vertex $u$ other than the source $s$ and the sink $t$, the total flow entering $u$ must equal the total flow leaving $u$. Formally, $\sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) = 0$ for all $u \in V - \{s, t\}$. This represents the "steady-state" property — no flow is created or destroyed at intermediate nodes.

(c) **Augmenting path:** Given a flow $f$, an augmenting path is a simple path $p$ from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the **residual network** $G_f$. The path's bottleneck $c_f(p) = \min\{c_f(u, v) : (u, v) \in p\}$ represents the additional flow that can be pushed from $s$ to $t$ through $p$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem. What is its significance in network flow problems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956) states that in any flow network, the maximum value of an $s$-$t$ flow equals the minimum capacity of an $s$-$t$ cut.

$$
\max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S, T)} c(S, T)
$$

**Explanation:** A **cut** $(S, T)$ is a partition of vertices with $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. The **capacity of the cut** is the sum of capacities of all edges crossing from $S$ to $T$. The theorem proves two things simultaneously:
1. The maximum flow cannot exceed the capacity of any cut.
2. The Ford-Fulkerson method produces a flow equal to the minimum cut when no augmenting path remains.

**Significance:**
- Provides a **certificate of optimality** — when no augmenting path exists, the current flow is provably maximum.
- Enables **bottleneck analysis** — the min-cut identifies the "weakest link" in the network.
- Forms the theoretical foundation for many combinatorial algorithms (matching, scheduling, image segmentation).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
> (a) **Define a flow network. State and prove the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem.** (7 marks)
> (b) **Apply the Ford-Fulkerson labeling algorithm to find the maximum flow from source $s$ to sink $t$ in the network shown below. Show all augmenting paths, residual graphs, and the final minimum cut.** (7 marks)

**Network:** $V = \{s, 1, 2, 3, 4, t\}$, with edges and capacities:
- $(s, 1) = 16, (s, 2) = 13$
- $(1, 2) = 10, (1, 3) = 12$
- $(2, 1) = 4, (2, 4) = 14$
- $(3, 2) = 9, (3, t) = 20$
- $(4, 3) = 7, (4, t) = 4$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow network definition and Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem (7 marks):**

A **flow network** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with capacity function $c: V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, a designated source $s$, and a designated sink $t$. A **flow** is a function $f: V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ satisfying:
1. **Capacity:** $f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$
2. **Skew symmetry:** $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$
3. **Conservation:** $\sum_{v} f(u, v) = 0$ for $u \neq s, t$

**Theorem:** $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T)$.

**Proof of $\max \vert f \vert \leq \min c(S, T)$:** For any cut $(S, T)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert f \vert &= f(s, V) \\
&= f(S, V) \quad \text{since } s \in S \text{ and flow within } S \text{ sums to 0} \\
&= f(S, S) + f(S, T) \\
&= 0 + f(S, T) \\
&\leq c(S, T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof of equality at termination:** When the algorithm halts, define $S$ = vertices reachable from $s$ in $G_f$, $T = V - S$. Then $s \in S$, $t \in T$. For every edge $(u, v)$ from $S$ to $T$, since $(u, v) \notin E_f$, $c_f(u, v) = 0$, so $f(u, v) = c(u, v)$. For edges from $T$ to $S$, $f(v, u) = 0$, so $f(u, v) = 0$. Thus:

$$
\vert f \vert = f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} f(u, v) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u, v) = c(S, T)
$$

Hence $\vert f \vert$ equals a cut capacity, so it equals the minimum.

**(b) Ford-Fulkerson application (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial flow $f = 0$.** BFS path: $s \to 1 \to 3 \to t$. Bottleneck: $\min(16, 12, 20) = 12$. Augment by 12.

Updated flow: $f(s,1) = 12, f(1,3) = 12, f(3,t) = 12$.

**Step 2.** BFS path: $s \to 2 \to 4 \to t$. Bottleneck: $\min(13, 14, 4) = 4$. Augment by 4.

Updated flow: $f(s,2) = 4, f(2,4) = 4, f(4,t) = 4$.

**Step 3.** BFS path: $s \to 2 \to 1 \to 3 \to t$. Check residuals: $c_f(s,2) = 9, c_f(2,1) = 4, c_f(1,3) = 0$ (saturated). Blocked. Try $s \to 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to t$: $c_f(s,1) = 4, c_f(1,2) = 10, c_f(2,4) = 10, c_f(4,3) = 7, c_f(3,t) = 8$. Bottleneck: $\min(4, 10, 10, 7, 8) = 4$. Augment by 4.

Updated flow: $f(s,1) = 16, f(1,2) = 4, f(2,4) = 8, f(4,3) = 4, f(3,t) = 16$.

**Step 4.** BFS path: $s \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to t$. Residuals: $c_f(s,2) = 9, c_f(2,4) = 6, c_f(4,3) = 3, c_f(3,t) = 4$. Bottleneck: $\min(9, 6, 3, 4) = 3$. Augment by 3.

Updated flow: $f(s,2) = 7, f(2,4) = 11, f(4,3) = 7, f(3,t) = 19$.

**Step 5.** BFS path: $s \to 2 \to 1 \to 3 \to t$. Residuals: $c_f(s,2) = 6, c_f(2,1) = 4, c_f(1,3) = 0$ — blocked. Try $s \to 2 \to 4 \to t$: $c_f(4,t) = 0$ — blocked.

Backward edge: $s \to 2 \to 1 \to 3 \to t$? No, $c_f(1,3) = 0$.

Check: $s \to 1$? $c_f(s,1) = 0$ — saturated.

So no more augmenting paths. **Maximum flow = $12 + 4 + 4 + 3 = 23$.**

**Minimum cut:** Reachable set $S = \{s, 1, 2, 4\}$ (since from $s$, $1, 2$ reachable; from $1$, $2$ already in $S$; from $2$, $4$ reachable; from $4$, $3$ requires $c_f(4,3) = 0$, so $3$ not in $S$).

$T = \{3, t\}$. Edges $S \to T$: $(1, 3) = 12, (2, 4)$ goes to $S$, $(4, 3) = 7$. Total: $12 + 7 = 19$? 

Wait, let me recompute. From $S = \{s, 1, 2, 4\}$, edges to $T = \{3, t\}$:
- $(1, 3) = 12$
- $(4, 3) = 7$ (but this is saturated, residual 0)
- $(4, t) = 4$ (saturated)

Cut capacity = $12 + 7 + 4 = 23$. ✓ Matches max flow.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks]**
> (a) **Explain the Ford-Fulkerson labeling algorithm with a neat example. Discuss its time complexity.** (7 marks)
> (b) **Differentiate between forward and backward edges in the residual network. How does the concept of backward edges ensure correctness of the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm? Use an illustrative example.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ford-Fulkerson Labeling Algorithm (7 marks):**

The labeling algorithm maintains three pieces of information for each labeled vertex $v$: (i) predecessor vertex, (ii) edge direction (+/−), and (iii) residual capacity (delta).

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** Label $s$ as $(-, -, \infty)$. Mark $s$ as scanned.
2. **Label phase:** For each scanned vertex $u$, examine all edges $(u, v)$ where $v$ is not yet labeled:
   - If $(u, v)$ has residual capacity $r > 0$ (forward edge), label $v$ as $(u, +, \min(r, \delta_u))$.
   - If $(v, u)$ is a backward edge with flow $f(v, u) > 0$, label $v$ as $(u, -, \min(f(v, u), \delta_u))$.
3. **Termination check:** If $t$ is labeled, go to step 4. If no more labels possible and $t$ unlabeled, terminate — max flow reached.
4. **Augmentation (change) phase:** Let $\Delta = \delta_t$. Trace back from $t$ to $s$ using labels:
   - For each "+" label on edge $(u, v)$: $f(u, v) \leftarrow f(u, v) + \Delta$.
   - For each "−" label on edge $(u, v)$: $f(u, v) \leftarrow f(u, v) - \Delta$.
5. **Erase all labels** and return to step 2.

**Example:** (Use the worked example from Section 3.2 above.)

**Time Complexity:**
- **Generic Ford-Fulkerson:** $O(E \cdot \vert f^{*} \vert)$ — pseudo-polynomial for integer capacities.
- **Edmonds-Karp (BFS-based):** $O(V \cdot E^2)$ — strongly polynomial.
- The BFS variant finds shortest augmenting paths, ensuring $O(VE)$ iterations.

**(b) Forward vs Backward Edges (7 marks):**

**Forward edges** in the residual network $G_f$ are edges $(u, v) \in E$ with $f(u, v) < c(u, v)$, i.e., spare capacity remains. These represent the ability to push **more** flow in the original direction.

**Backward edges** in $G_f$ are edges $(v, u)$ for $(u, v) \in E$ with $f(u, v) > 0$. These represent the ability to **cancel** or **redirect** flow that was previously assigned — equivalent to reducing $f(u, v)$.

**Why backward edges are essential for correctness:**

Consider this scenario: an initial bad choice in the augmenting path leads to suboptimal flow. Without backward edges, we cannot undo the mistake.

**Example:** Network with $s \to a \to t$ (capacity 1), $s \to b \to t$ (capacity 1), and $a \to b$ (capacity 1).

- Suppose we first augment along $s \to a \to b \to t$ with flow 1. Now $f(s,a) = 1, f(a,b) = 1, f(b,t) = 1$. Value = 1.
- Next augment: $s \to b \to t$ — but $f(b,t) = 1$ saturates this edge. Cannot proceed.
- We need to cancel the flow on $a \to b$ to free up $s \to a \to t$. Using **backward edge** $b \to a$, we augment $s \to a \leftarrow b \to t$? No, we use $s \to a$ (forward) and $b \to a$ (backward, meaning reduce $f(a,b)$) and $s \to b$ (forward) and $b \to t$ (forward) — wait, this doesn't form a path.

Correct augmenting path: $s \to b$ (forward residual = 1), $b \to a$ (backward, cancels $f(a,b) = 1$), $a \to t$ (forward residual = 1). Bottleneck = 1. Augment: $f(s,b) = 1, f(a,b) = 0, f(a,t) = 1$. New total flow = 2 (max).

Without the backward edge, the algorithm would be stuck at value 1 — a suboptimal solution. **Backward edges thus guarantee the greedy process can correct earlier mistakes, ensuring global optimality.**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting skew symmetry:** When computing net flow through a vertex, students often sum all outgoing edges without subtracting incoming flow. Use the formal sum $\sum_v f(u, v)$, which automatically handles sign.
> 2. **Confusing residual capacity with original capacity:** After augmentation, the residual network **changes**. Students frequently use original capacities in subsequent steps. Always recompute $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$.
> 3. **Missing reverse residual edges:** When $f(u, v) > 0$, the reverse edge $(v, u)$ has residual capacity $f(u, v)$ in $G_f$. Omitting this loses the ability to "undo" suboptimal augmentations — and costs you 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Improper min-cut identification:** The min-cut is found by computing reachable vertices $S$ from $s$ in the **final** residual network. Many students incorrectly list saturated edges without verifying reachability.
> 5. **Not verifying flow conservation:** At each step, $\vert f \vert$ measured at $s$ should equal $\vert f \vert$ measured at $t$. Failing this check is a sign of computational error.
> 6. **Ignoring integer capacity assumption:** The $O(E f_{\max})$ bound assumes integer capacities. For real-valued capacities, Ford-Fulkerson may not terminate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Flow network:** Directed graph with source $s$, sink $t$, and non-negative edge capacities.
- [ ] **Flow constraints:** Capacity $f \leq c$, skew symmetry $f(u,v) = -f(v,u)$, conservation at interior vertices.
- [ ] **Value of flow:** Net flow out of source: $\vert f \vert = \sum_v f(s, v)$.
- [ ] **Residual capacity:** $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$. Edge present in $G_f$ iff $c_f > 0$.
- [ ] **Augmenting path:** $s \to t$ path in $G_f$. Bottleneck = minimum $c_f$ along path.
- [ ] **Augmentation rule:** Forward edge → add $\Delta$; Backward edge → subtract $\Delta$.
- [ ] **Termination:** When no $s$-$t$ path exists in $G_f$, current flow is **maximum**.
- [ ] **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:** $\max \vert f \vert = \min_{(S,T)} c(S, T)$. **Most important theorem.**
- [ ] **Min-cut identification:** $S$ = reachable from $s$ in $G_f$ at termination; $T = V - S$.
- [ ] **Cut capacity formula:** Sum of **original** capacities of edges from $S$ to $T$ (not flow).
- [ ] **Ford-Fulkerson complexity:** $O(E \cdot \vert f^{*} \vert)$ integer; $O(V E^2)$ with Edmonds-Karp BFS.
- [ ] **Time complexity corner cases:** BFS variant guarantees polynomial time; DFS may loop on irrational capacities.
- [ ] **Labeling algorithm labels:** Each vertex gets (predecessor, +/− direction, delta/bottleneck).
- [ ] **Three phases:** (1) Initialize, (2) Label vertices, (3) Augment or terminate.
- [ ] **Applications:** Bipartite matching, network reliability, project scheduling, image segmentation, transportation.
- [ ] **Common KTU question patterns:** Trace algorithm on given graph, prove Max-Flow Min-Cut, identify min-cut, compare with cut capacity.

> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Formal Statement)**
> In any **flow network**, the **maximum value of a flow** from a source vertex $s$ to a sink vertex $t$ is **exactly equal** to the **minimum capacity of an $s$-$t$ cut** (i.e., a partition of the vertices that separates $s$ from $t$).

Mathematically, if $G = (V, E)$ is a flow network with source $s$ and sink $t$, and $c: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is the capacity function, then:

$$\max_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S, T) \in \mathcal{C}} c(S, T)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{F}$ = set of all feasible $s$-$t$ flows
- $\mathcal{C}$ = set of all $s$-$t$ cuts
- $\vert f \vert$ = value of flow $f$
- $c(S, T)$ = capacity of cut $(S, T)$

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Prerequisites)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Flow Network** | A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative **capacity** $c(u, v) \geq 0$. |
| **Source ($s$)** | A designated vertex where flow originates; has no incoming edges. |
| **Sink ($t$)** | A designated vertex where flow terminates; has no outgoing edges. |
| **Flow** | A function $f: V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ satisfying capacity, skew-symmetry, and flow conservation constraints. |
| **Cut $(S, T)$** | A partition of $V$ into $S$ and $T = V \setminus S$ such that $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. |
| **Cut Capacity** | $c(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u, v)$ — sum of capacities of edges going from $S$ to $T$. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pipeline" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Water Distribution Network**
> Imagine a city water pipeline system where water flows from a **reservoir (source $s$)** to a **city (sink $t$)** through interconnected pipes of varying widths (capacities). The **maximum flow** is the greatest amount of water that can be delivered per second. The **minimum cut** is the *bottleneck* — the thinnest set of pipes whose complete removal would disconnect the reservoir from the city. **Theorem insight**: The city's maximum water supply is limited precisely by its narrowest bottleneck. No matter how wide you make other pipes, you cannot exceed the flow through the narrowest chokepoint.

**Engineering Insight**: This is why telecom engineers, traffic planners, and supply chain managers obsess over *bottlenecks* — the max-flow min-cut theorem formally proves that system throughput is governed by the weakest cross-section.

## 1.4 The Three Equivalent Conditions (Fundamental Equivalence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem establishes that the following three statements are **equivalent** for any flow $f$ in a network $G$:
> 
> 1. $f$ is a **maximum flow** in $G$.
> 2. The **residual network** $G_f$ contains **no augmenting path** from $s$ to $t$.
> 3. There exists an $s$-$t$ cut $(S, T)$ such that $\vert f \vert = c(S, T)$ (i.e., $f$ saturates a minimum cut).

This triple equivalence is the cornerstone used in both the proof of the theorem and in algorithmic design (Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp, Dinic's algorithm).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cut Theorem (Lemma 1) — Foundational Result

> [!NOTE]
> **Cut Theorem**: For any flow $f$ and any cut $(S, T)$:
> $$\vert f \vert = f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u)$$

**Why this matters**: This identity states that the *flow value* of $f$ equals the *net flow* across *every* cut. Combined with the fact that $f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$ and $f(v, u) \geq 0$ (for $u \in S, v \in T$), we get the **capacity bound**: $\vert f \vert \leq c(S, T)$.

## 2.2 The Three Logical Implications of the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem

The theorem is proven by establishing a **cyclic chain of implications**:

1. **($\Rightarrow$) Maximum flow $\Rightarrow$ No augmenting path.**
   If $f$ is maximum, suppose $G_f$ had an augmenting path $p$. Then $f \oplus p$ would be a larger flow — contradiction.

2. **($\Rightarrow$) No augmenting path $\Rightarrow$ Some cut is saturated.**
   If $G_f$ has no $s$-$t$ path, define $S = \{v \in V : \text{there exists a path from } s \text{ to } v \text{ in } G_f\}$ and $T = V \setminus S$. Then $s \in S$, $t \in T$, and every edge $(u, v)$ with $u \in S, v \in T$ must be **saturated** ($f(u,v) = c(u,v)$), while every reverse edge must carry zero flow. Hence $\vert f \vert = f(S, T) = c(S, T)$.

3. **($\Rightarrow$) Some cut saturated $\Rightarrow$ $f$ is maximum.**
   By the Cut Theorem, for any flow $f'$ and any cut $(S, T)$: $\vert f' \vert \leq c(S, T)$. If $\vert f \vert = c(S, T)$ for some cut, then $f$ is a maximum flow.

## 2.3 Ford-Fulkerson Method (Algorithmic Backbone)

```
FORD-FULKERSON(G, s, t):
  1. for each edge (u,v) ∈ E(G):
  2.     f(u,v) ← 0, f(v,u) ← 0
  3. while there exists a path p from s to t in residual graph G_f:
  4.     c_f(p) ← min { c_f(u,v) : (u,v) ∈ p }
  5.     for each edge (u,v) ∈ p:
  6.         f(u,v) ← f(u,v) + c_f(p)
  7.         f(v,u) ← f(v,u) - c_f(p)
  8. return f
```

- **Residual capacity**: $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ if $(u, v) \in E$; $c_f(u, v) = f(v, u)$ if $(v, u) \in E$; else $0$.
- **Bottleneck capacity** of path $p$: $c_f(p) = \min\{c_f(u,v) : (u,v) \in p\}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $0 \leq f(u,v) \leq c(u,v)$ | **Capacity constraint** | Flow feasibility |
| 2 | $f(u,v) = -f(v,u)$ | **Skew-symmetry** | Reverse flow bookkeeping |
| 3 | $\sum_{v} f(u,v) = 0$ for $u \neq s,t$ | **Flow conservation** | Internal vertices |
| 4 | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v} f(s,v)$ | **Flow value** | Net outflow from source |
| 5 | $f(S,T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} f(u,v) - f(v,u)$ | **Net flow across cut** | Cut analysis |
| 6 | $\vert f \vert \leq c(S, T)$ | **Capacity bound** | Upper bound on flow |
| 7 | $c_f(u,v) = c(u,v) - f(u,v)$ | **Residual capacity** | Ford-Fulkerson step |
| 8 | $c_f(p) = \min\{c_f(u,v) : (u,v) \in p\}$ | **Bottleneck** | Augmentation amount |
| 9 | $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S,T)$ | **THEOREM** | Maximum flow value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Absolute Values in Tables**: Throughout the formula sheet, $\vert f \vert$ denotes the *value of the flow* (a scalar), not absolute value. To avoid breaking markdown table syntax, the vertical bar has been rendered as part of standard LaTeX inside a math context. Always use the $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation for *flow value* to distinguish it from *cardinality* $\mid \cdot \mid$ in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Telecommunications**: Maximum data rate between two routers in a network with bandwidth-limited links.
2. **Transportation**: Maximum number of vehicles/people that can travel from origin to destination per unit time.
3. **Bipartite Matching**: Modeled as max-flow — finds maximum job assignments, marriage pairings, network bandwidth allocation.
4. **Image Segmentation (Computer Vision)**: Min-cut separates foreground from background.
5. **Supply Chain & Logistics**: Maximum goods that can be shipped given warehouse/transport capacity limits.
6. **VLSI Circuit Design**: Routing problems, wire congestion minimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Proof of the Cut Theorem

**Theorem Statement**: For any flow $f$ and any cut $(S, T)$ with $s \in S, t \in T$:

$$f(S, T) = \vert f \vert$$

**Proof (Line by Line)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(S, T) &= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u)
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition of net flow across a cut.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u)
\end{aligned}
$$

Partition $V$ into $S \cup T$, split the first double sum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v) - \sum_{v \in S} \sum_{u \in T} f(u, v)
\end{aligned}
$$

Swap the indices $u \leftrightarrow v$ in the last term (sum is symmetric over index labels).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(v, u)
\end{aligned}
$$

Rename indices back to $(u, v)$ in the last term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} \left[ f(u, v) + f(v, u) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Combine the two $S$-internal sums (they are both summed over $u,v \in S$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} 0
\end{aligned}
$$

By **flow conservation** at every $u \in S \setminus \{s\}$, $\sum_{v} f(u,v) = 0$, and the $S$-internal sum $f(u,v) + f(v,u) = 0$ by skew-symmetry — so the entire second term vanishes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v)
\end{aligned}
$$

After applying conservation, only the source $s$ contributes (since $s$ is the only vertex in $S$ with non-zero net flow).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v) = \vert f \vert \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition of flow value as the net outflow from the source $s$.

## 3.2 Capacity Bound Derivation

For any $u \in S$ and $v \in T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(u, v) &\leq c(u, v) & \text{(capacity constraint)} \\
-f(v, u) &\leq 0 \quad &\text{(since } f(v, u) \geq 0 \text{ by capacity constraint on edge } (v,u)) 
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing across all $u \in S, v \in T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(S, T) &= \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} f(v, u) \\
&\leq \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u, v) - 0 = c(S, T)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\vert f \vert = f(S, T) \leq c(S, T) \quad \text{for every cut } (S, T)$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Min-Cut Identification from Max-Flow

**Problem**: Given the network below with capacities, find the max-flow and corresponding min-cut using Ford-Fulkerson.

**Network**:
- $s \to a$: capacity **10**
- $s \to b$: capacity **8**
- $a \to b$: capacity **5**
- $a \to t$: capacity **15**
- $b \to t$: capacity **10**

**Step 1 — Augmenting Path 1**: $s \to a \to t$
Bottleneck: $\min(10, 15) = 10$. Flow $f_1 = 10$. Update: $s\to a$ saturated.

**Step 2 — Augmenting Path 2**: $s \to b \to t$
Bottleneck: $\min(8, 10) = 8$. Flow $f_2 = 8$. Update: $s \to b$ saturated.

**Step 3 — Augmenting Path 3**: $s \to a \leftarrow b \to t$ (using reverse edge $b \to a$ in residual)
Bottleneck: $\min(0, 5, 2) = 0$ — **no augmenting path exists** since $s \to a$ has zero residual capacity.

Wait — re-check. After step 1: $f(s,a) = 10$, $c_f(s,a) = 0$. So $s \to a$ is blocked. Re-examine path: $s \to b \to a \to t$ requires residual on $b \to a = f(a,b) - 0$. But $f(a,b) = 0$ initially, so $c_f(b,a) = 0$. Thus no augmenting path.

**Maximum Flow Value**: $\vert f \vert = 10 + 8 = 18$

**Min-Cut Identification**:
- $S$ = vertices reachable from $s$ in $G_f$ = $\{s\}$
- $T = \{a, b, t\}$
- Edges from $S$ to $T$: $s \to a$ (cap 10) + $s \to b$ (cap 8) = **18**
- Therefore: $c(S, T) = 18 = \vert f \vert$ ✓ — **minimum cut verified**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Verification Table

| Property | Maximum Flow $f^*$ | Minimum Cut $(S^*, T^*)$ | Equality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flow value / Cut capacity | $\vert f^* \vert = 18$ | $c(S^*, T^*) = 18$ | ✓ Equal |
| Saturated forward edges | $s \to a, s \to b$ | Both crossing the cut | All $S \to T$ edges saturated |
| Zero reverse edges in cut | $b \to a, t \to a$ (none) | $f(v, u) = 0$ for $v \in T, u \in S$ | Verified |
| Augmenting path in $G_{f^*}$ | None exists | Cut partitions reachable set | Consistent with theory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Network Architecture (Original Network)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S))
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeT((T))
    nodeS -- "10" --> nodeA
    nodeS -- "8" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "5" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "15" --> nodeT
    nodeB -- "10" --> nodeT
```

**Description**: Source $S$ connects to intermediate vertices $A$ and $B$, both of which connect to sink $T$. Edge labels represent capacities. This is the input flow network $G$ for the Max-Flow Min-Cut analysis.

## 4.2 Residual Network After Max-Flow Computation

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S))
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeT((T))
    nodeS -- "0/10" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "10/10" --> nodeS
    nodeS -- "0/8" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "8/8" --> nodeS
    nodeA -- "5/5" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "5/5" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "5/15" --> nodeT
    nodeT -- "10/15" --> nodeA
    nodeB -- "2/10" --> nodeT
    nodeT -- "8/10" --> nodeB
```

**Description**: Each edge is labeled in `flow/capacity` format. The residual graph $G_f$ has **no path** from $S$ to $T$, confirming that $f$ is a maximum flow. Edges with `flow = capacity` (saturated) cannot be traversed forward; only reverse edges offer residual capacity.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ford-Fulkerson

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA[Input: Flow Network G, Source s, Sink t] --> blockB[Initialize: f u,v = 0 for all edges]
    blockB --> blockC[Construct Residual Graph G_f]
    blockC --> blockD{Augmenting Path s to t in G_f?}
    blockD -- Yes --> blockE[Compute Bottleneck: c_f of path]
    blockE --> blockF[Augment Flow: f = f plus c_f p]
    blockF --> blockC
    blockD -- No --> blockG[Output: Maximum Flow f]
    blockG --> blockH[Build Reachability Set S from s in G_f]
    blockH --> blockI[Min Cut: Edges from S to T, T = V minus S]
    blockI --> blockJ[Output: Min Cut S, T and Capacity c S,T]
```

**Description**: This block diagram traces the complete Ford-Fulkerson algorithm pipeline from input network through iterative augmentation to the dual output of (i) maximum flow and (ii) minimum cut. The loop `blockC → blockD → blockE → blockF → blockC` represents the iterative refinement until no augmenting path exists.

## 4.4 Min-Cut Partition Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SET_S["SET S  Source Side"]
        nodeS2((S))
    end
    subgraph SET_T["SET T  Sink Side"]
        nodeA2((A))
        nodeB2((B))
        nodeT2((T))
    end
    nodeS2 -. "Capacity 10: S to A" .-> nodeA2
    nodeS2 -. "Capacity 8: S to B" .-> nodeB2
```

**Description**: The minimum cut $(S, T) = (\{s\}, \{a, b, t\})$ partitions the graph into two sets. The dotted edges represent the cut boundary — these are the *only* edges crossing from $S$ to $T$. Total cut capacity: $10 + 8 = 18$, which equals the maximum flow value, confirming the theorem.

## 4.5 Time Complexity Comparison Table

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Path Selection Strategy | Suitable For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ford-Fulkerson** | $O(E \cdot \vert f^* \vert)$ | Arbitrary augmenting path | Integer capacities |
| **Edmonds-Karp** | $O(V \cdot E^2)$ | BFS (shortest path) | General use |
| **Dinic's** | $O(V^2 \cdot E)$ | BFS + blocking flows | Dense graphs |
| **Push-Relabel** | $O(V^3)$ | Local relabeling | High-performance |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem states that in any flow network, the **maximum value of a flow** from a source $s$ to a sink $t$ is equal to the **minimum capacity of an $s$-$t$ cut**. Formally:

$$\max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S,T)} c(S,T)$$

An $s$-$t$ cut $(S, T)$ is a partition of vertices such that $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. The theorem has three equivalent characterizations: (1) $f$ is maximum, (2) residual graph $G_f$ has no $s$-$t$ path, (3) $\vert f \vert = c(S,T)$ for some cut. This result is fundamental in network optimization, bipartite matching, and resource allocation problems.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series II]
**Q: Define residual network. How is it used in Ford-Fulkerson algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A **residual network** $G_f = (V, E_f)$ of a flow network $G$ under flow $f$ is a directed graph where each edge $(u, v) \in E$ gives rise to a residual capacity $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ (and $c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$ for reverse edges). It represents the *remaining* capacity available for additional flow.

In Ford-Fulkerson, augmenting paths are found in $G_f$ (not in $G$). The bottleneck $c_f(p) = \min\{c_f(u,v) : (u,v) \in p\}$ is added to the flow along $p$. The process repeats until $G_f$ has no $s$-$t$ path, at which point the current flow is maximum. The residual network thus serves as the **computational engine** for iterative flow augmentation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often forget that a *cut capacity* counts only edges from $S$ to $T$ — not bidirectional. Including reverse edge capacities **loses 2–3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing flow value $\vert f \vert$ with edge flow $f(u,v)$. The flow value is the *net* outflow from source.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Failing to construct the **residual network correctly** — using $c(u,v) - f(u,v)$ is correct; using $c(u,v)$ directly is a fundamental error that loses 4–5 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Incomplete proof of equivalence — only stating "max = min" without the three-condition chain. The KTU 2024 scheme mandates the equivalence argument.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Not drawing the **min-cut** explicitly with the partition $S$ and $T$ sets — lose 2 marks even if the value is correct.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** For the given flow network, compute the **maximum flow** from $s$ to $t$ using the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. Show all augmenting paths, residual capacities, and intermediate flows. **(7 marks)**

**Network**:
- $s \to v_1$: cap 16
- $s \to v_2$: cap 13
- $v_1 \to v_2$: cap 10
- $v_2 \to v_1$: cap 4
- $v_1 \to t$: cap 12
- $v_2 \to t$: cap 14

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — Path $p_1: s \to v_1 \to t$**
Bottleneck: $\min(16, 12) = 12$
Update: $f(s, v_1) = 12$, $f(v_1, t) = 12$, $f(t, v_1) = -12$, $f(v_1, s) = -12$

**Step 2 — Path $p_2: s \to v_2 \to t$**
Bottleneck: $\min(13, 14) = 13$
Update: $f(s, v_2) = 13$, $f(v_2, t) = 13$, $f(t, v_2) = -13$, $f(v_2, s) = -13$

**Step 3 — Path $p_3: s \to v_1 \to v_2 \to t$**
Bottleneck: $\min(16-12, 10, 14-13) = \min(4, 10, 1) = 1$
Update: $f(s, v_1) = 13$, $f(v_1, v_2) = 1$, $f(v_2, t) = 14$

**Step 4 — Check residual graph for augmenting paths**:
- $s \to v_1$: residual = $16 - 13 = 3$
- $s \to v_2$: residual = $13 - 13 = 0$ (saturated)
- $v_1 \to v_2$: residual = $10 - 1 = 9$
- $v_2 \to v_1$: residual = $4 + 1 = 5$ (reverse)
- $v_1 \to t$: residual = $12 - 12 = 0$ (saturated)
- $v_2 \to t$: residual = $14 - 14 = 0$ (saturated)

No path from $s$ to $t$ remains — algorithm terminates.

**Maximum Flow Value**: $\vert f \vert = 12 + 13 = 25$ (or equivalently $f(v_1, t) + f(v_2, t) = 12 + 14 - 1 = 25$) ✓

**Q: (b)** From the maximum flow obtained in part (a), identify the **minimum cut** and prove using the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem that this cut has capacity equal to the max flow value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — Build reachability set in $G_f$**:
Starting from $s$, traverse residual graph:
- From $s$: $s \to v_1$ (residual 3) ✓ reachable
- From $v_1$: $v_1 \to v_2$ (residual 9) ✓ reachable; $v_1 \to t$ blocked (residual 0)
- From $v_2$: $v_2 \to v_1$ (residual 5) — already in $S$; no other outgoing residual
- $t$ is **not** reachable from $s$

Therefore: $S = \{s, v_1, v_2\}$ and $T = \{t\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute cut capacity $c(S, T)$**:
Edges from $S$ to $T$:
- $v_1 \to t$: capacity = 12
- $v_2 \to t$: capacity = 14
- $s \to t$: no such edge

$$c(S, T) = 12 + 14 = 26$$

Wait — verify: max flow was 25, but cut is 26? Let me recheck.

**Corrected analysis**: After step 3, $f(v_1, t) = 12$ (saturated) and $f(v_2, t) = 14$ (saturated). Total flow = $12 + 13 = 25$, but the cut $c(\{s, v_1, v_2\}, \{t\}) = 12 + 14 = 26$.

**Re-examination**: We need to identify the *correct* min cut. Re-examine: is $v_2$ reachable in $G_f$?
- $s \to v_1$: residual 3 ✓
- $v_1 \to v_2$: residual 9 ✓
- So $S = \{s, v_1, v_2\}$, $T = \{t\}$.

Cut capacity: $c(S, T) = 12 + 14 = 26$. But max flow = 25. **Discrepancy indicates error in path enumeration**.

**Re-do Step 3 carefully**:
- After step 2: $f(s, v_2) = 13$, $f(v_2, t) = 13$. Residual: $c_f(v_2, t) = 1$, $c_f(s, v_2) = 0$.
- Path $s \to v_1 \to v_2 \to t$: bottleneck = $\min(4, 10, 1) = 1$.

After this, $f(v_2, t) = 14$ (saturated). Total = $12 + 13 + 1 = 26$. **Correction**: max flow = 26.

**Final max flow**: $\vert f \vert = 26$, min cut $(S, T) = (\{s, v_1, v_2\}, \{t\})$ with $c(S, T) = 12 + 14 = 26$ ✓

**Step 3 — Apply the theorem**:
By the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T) = 26$. The cut is verified — the bottleneck of the network is precisely the two edges entering $t$, both of which are saturated. This confirms that no greater flow can be pushed without increasing the capacity of edges crossing this cut.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — *Alternative Choice*

**Q: (a)** State and prove the **Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem** rigorously. Show the three equivalent conditions and the chain of implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)**:

**Statement**: For a flow network $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$, sink $t$, and capacity function $c$, the maximum $s$-$t$ flow value equals the minimum $s$-$t$ cut capacity.

**Proof Outline** — Three equivalent statements:
1. $f$ is a maximum flow in $G$.
2. The residual network $G_f$ contains no augmenting path.
3. $\vert f \vert = c(S, T)$ for some cut $(S, T)$.

**Implication 1 ⇒ 2**: If $f$ is maximum, suppose $G_f$ has augmenting path $p$. Augmenting yields $f' = f \oplus p$ with $\vert f' \vert = \vert f \vert + c_f(p) > \vert f \vert$, contradicting maximality.

**Implication 2 ⇒ 3**: If no $s$-$t$ path in $G_f$, let $S = \{v \in V : s \leadsto v \text{ in } G_f\}$ and $T = V \setminus S$. Then $s \in S, t \in T$. For any $u \in S, v \in T$: edge $(u, v)$ must be saturated ($f(u,v) = c(u,v)$), else $c_f(u,v) > 0$ would put $v$ in $S$. Similarly $f(v, u) = 0$. Hence:

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} f(u,v) - f(v,u) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u,v) = c(S, T)$$

By the Cut Theorem, $\vert f \vert = f(S, T) = c(S, T)$.

**Implication 3 ⇒ 1**: For any flow $f'$ and any cut $(S, T)$: $\vert f' \vert \leq c(S, T)$. If $\vert f \vert = c(S, T)$, then $f$ achieves the upper bound and is therefore maximum.

The cyclic chain $1 \Rightarrow 2 \Rightarrow 3 \Rightarrow 1$ completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

**Q: (b)** Explain the **Ford-Fulkerson algorithm** with a clear description of the residual network, augmenting paths, and termination condition. What is its time complexity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)**:

**Algorithm Description**:
Ford-Fulkerson iteratively augments a flow by finding paths in the *residual network* $G_f$ — a derived graph that indicates how much additional flow each edge can carry.

**Residual Network**: For each edge $(u, v) \in E$:
- $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ (remaining forward capacity)
- $c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$ (residual reverse capacity allowing flow "cancellation")

**Augmenting Path**: A path $p$ from $s$ to $t$ in $G_f$. Every edge on $p$ has $c_f > 0$.

**Algorithm Steps**:
1. Initialize $f(u, v) = 0$ for all edges.
2. While augmenting path $p$ exists in $G_f$:
   - Compute bottleneck: $c_f(p) = \min\{c_f(u, v) : (u, v) \in p\}$
   - Augment: $f(u, v) \leftarrow f(u, v) + c_f(p)$ for forward edges; $f(v, u) \leftarrow f(v, u) - c_f(p)$ for reverse edges.
3. Return maximum flow $f$.

**Termination Condition**: The algorithm halts when $G_f$ has no $s$-$t$ path. By the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, the resulting flow is maximum.

**Time Complexity**:
- For integer capacities: $O(E \cdot \vert f^* \vert)$ where $\vert f^* \vert$ is the max flow value. Each iteration augments by at least 1 unit.
- For rational capacities: scales similarly after multiplying by common denominator.
- Edmonds-Karp variant (BFS path selection): $O(V \cdot E^2)$ — polynomial in network size.

**Key Insight**: Ford-Fulkerson is a *method*; choosing augmenting paths via BFS gives Edmonds-Karp; DFS gives the original Ford-Fulkerson. The choice affects performance but not correctness.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem**
> 
> - **Core Theorem**: $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T)$ — the golden identity of network flow.
> - **Three Equivalent Conditions**: Max flow ⟺ No augmenting path ⟺ Saturated min cut.
> - **Residual Capacity**: $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ for forward; $c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$ for reverse.
> - **Cut Definition**: Edges counted only in $S \to T$ direction for cut capacity.
> - **Flow Value**: $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v} f(s, v)$ — net outflow at source.
> - **Cut Theorem (Lemma)**: $\vert f \vert = f(S, T)$ for *any* cut — the net flow across every cut equals flow value.
> - **Capacity Bound**: $\vert f \vert \leq c(S, T)$ — corollary of Cut Theorem and capacity constraint.
> - **Algorithm**: Ford-Fulkerson = iterative augmentation via residual paths; terminates at max flow.
> - **Edmonds-Karp Enhancement**: BFS path selection gives $O(VE^2)$ — polynomial guarantee.
> - **Skew-Symmetry**: $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$ — enables reverse edge flow in residual network.
> - **Flow Conservation**: Net flow at every internal vertex = 0; only $s$ and $t$ have non-zero net flow.
> - **Common Exam Trap**: Forgetting to count cut capacity in $S \to T$ direction only (NOT both directions).
> - **Min-Cut Identification**: $S$ = vertices reachable from $s$ in $G_f$ after max flow; $T = V \setminus S$.
> - **Applications**: Bipartite matching, network reliability, image segmentation, transportation, telecom routing.
> - **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level**: Definition questions at L1/L2; proof/derivation at L3/L4; algorithmic application at L4/L5.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Menger's Proof Using Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Menger's Proof Using Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Statement of Menger's Theorem (Vertex Version)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Menger's Theorem — Vertex-Disjoint Version (1927)**
>
> Let $G = (V, E)$ be a finite, undirected, connected graph and let $s, t \in V$ be two **non-adjacent** vertices. Then the **maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths** in $G$ is equal to the **minimum number of vertices** whose removal disconnects $s$ from $t$.

**Notation used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus:**

$$
\kappa_{s,t}(G) \;=\; \lambda_{s,t}(G)
$$

where:
- $\kappa_{s,t}(G)$ = vertex-connectivity between $s$ and $t$ = minimum size of a vertex separator.
- $\lambda_{s,t}(G)$ = maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths.

### 1.2 Formal Statement of the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Ford & Fulkerson, 1956)**
>
> In any single-source single-sink flow network, the **maximum value of a flow** from source $s$ to sink $t$ equals the **minimum capacity of an $s$–$t$ cut**.

$$
\max_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \vert f \vert \;=\; \min_{C \in \mathcal{C}_{s,t}} c(C)
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Why Menger's Theorem Matters

Imagine a city's **water-pipeline network** where water must travel from a **reservoir** $s$ to a **treatment plant** $t$.

- Each **pumping station** (vertex) can handle only **one pipe at a time**.
- The city engineer asks: *"How many independent backup pipelines can I lay?"* — this is the **maximum number of vertex-disjoint paths**.
- A saboteur asks: *"What is the minimum number of pumping stations I must blow up to cut off water supply?"* — this is the **minimum vertex separator**.

> **Menger's Theorem guarantees these two numbers are ALWAYS EQUAL.**

The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem is the *engine* used to prove this beautiful equality.

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Network Reliability** | Finding minimum link/node failure for system breakdown |
| **VLSI Circuit Design** | Layer assignment and via minimization |
| **Transportation** | Robustness of road/rail networks |
| **Cybersecurity** | Identifying critical nodes in a communication graph |
| **Parallel Computing** | Finding disjoint routing channels |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-requisite Definitions for the Proof

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 1 — Flow Network**
> A directed graph $N = (V, E)$ with a **source** $s$, a **sink** $t$, and a **capacity function** $c : E \to \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ on every directed edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 — $s$–$t$ Flow**
> A function $f : E \to \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ satisfying:
> 1. **Capacity Constraint:** $0 \le f(e) \le c(e)$ for every $e \in E$.
> 2. **Conservation Constraint:** For every $v \in V \setminus \{s, t\}$,
> $$\sum_{(u,v) \in E} f(u, v) \;=\; \sum_{(v, w) \in E} f(v, w)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 3 — $s$–$t$ Cut**
> A partition $(S, T)$ of $V$ with $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. The **capacity** of the cut is:
> $$c(S, T) \;=\; \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T, \, (u,v) \in E} c(u, v)$$

### 2.2 Logical Roadmap of the Proof

The classical KTU-board proof converts the **undirected connectivity problem** into a **directed flow problem** in **four rigorous stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Vertex Splitting:** Replace every vertex $v$ with two nodes $(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}})$ joined by a unit-capacity edge.
- **Stage 2 — Edge Orientation:** Replace every undirected edge $\{u, v\}$ with two oppositely directed arcs, each of capacity **1**.
- **Stage 3 — Apply Max-Flow Min-Cut:** The resulting directed network admits an integer-valued max flow (Integrality Theorem).
- **Stage 4 — Equivalence Mapping:** Show that max flow = max vertex-disjoint paths AND min cut = min vertex separator.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v)$ | Value of flow leaving source $s$ |
| 2 | $\sum_{(u,v) \in E} f(u,v) - \sum_{(v,w) \in E} f(v,w) = b(v)$ | Net flow balance at $v$ |
| 3 | $c(S, T) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E, u \in S, v \in T} c(u,v)$ | Capacity of cut $(S, T)$ |
| 4 | $\kappa_{s,t}(G) = \lambda_{s,t}(G)$ | Menger's equality (vertex version) |
| 5 | $\kappa'(s,t) = \lambda'(s,t)$ | Menger's equality (edge version) |
| 6 | $c(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) = 1$ | Split-edge capacity in construction |
| 7 | $c(u_{\text{out}}, v_{\text{in}}) = c(v_{\text{out}}, u_{\text{in}}) = 1$ | Directed arc capacities |
| 8 | $f(e) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Integrality property of max flow |
| 9 | $\vert f^{*} \vert = c(S^{*}, T^{*})$ | Max-flow = Min-cut (optimality) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This reduction is the foundation for solving the **minimum vertex cut** problem using **polynomial-time flow algorithms** such as Edmonds–Karp, Dinic's, and Push–Relabel. KTU examiners frequently test this reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Construction of the Auxiliary Flow Network $N(G)$

**Given:** Undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with distinct non-adjacent vertices $s, t \in V$.

**Construct:** A directed flow network $N(G) = (V', E', c)$ as follows.

**Step A — Vertex Splitting (Every $v \in V$ produces $v_{\text{in}}$ and $v_{\text{out}}$):**

$$
V' \;=\; \big\{ v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}} \mid v \in V \big\}
$$

**Step B — Insert Unit-Capacity Split Edges:**

$$
E'_{1} \;=\; \big\{ (v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) \mid v \in V \big\}, \quad c(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) \;=\; 1
$$

**Step C — Replace Every Undirected Edge $\{u, v\} \in E$ with Two Directed Arcs:**

$$
E'_{2} \;=\; \big\{ (u_{\text{out}}, v_{\text{in}}), (v_{\text{out}}, u_{\text{in}}) \mid \{u, v\} \in E \big\}
$$

$$
c(u_{\text{out}}, v_{\text{in}}) \;=\; c(v_{\text{out}}, u_{\text{in}}) \;=\; 1
$$

**Step D — Set Source and Sink:** Keep $s_{\text{out}}$ as the source and $t_{\text{in}}$ as the sink of $N(G)$. *(Equivalently, one can pick $s_{\text{in}}$ and $t_{\text{out}}$.)*

### 3.2 Lemma 1 — Flow to Vertex-Disjoint Paths

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Claim:** Every integer $s$–$t$ flow of value $k$ in $N(G)$ corresponds to exactly $k$ internally vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths in $G$.

**Proof (Exhaustive):**

Assume $f$ is an integer flow of value $k$ in $N(G)$. Since all capacities are **1**, the integrality theorem guarantees an integer-valued max flow.

For each unit of flow from $s_{\text{out}}$ to $t_{\text{in}}$, trace the **unique path** of flow-1 arcs:

$$
P_i \;=\; s_{\text{out}} \to v^{(1)}_{\text{in}} \to v^{(1)}_{\text{out}} \to v^{(2)}_{\text{in}} \to v^{(2)}_{\text{out}} \to \cdots \to t_{\text{in}}
$$

**Why vertex-disjointness holds:**

Suppose two such paths $P_i$ and $P_j$ share an internal vertex $v$. Then both paths must traverse the **split edge** $(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}})$, and since

$$
c(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) \;=\; 1
$$

the total flow through this edge is at most **1**, contradicting the assumption that both paths use $v$.

$$
\therefore \quad \text{All } P_i \text{ are internally vertex-disjoint in } G.
$$

Each $P_i$ corresponds to a unique $s$–$t$ path in $G$ by **collapsing** each pair $(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}})$ back to $v$.

### 3.3 Lemma 2 — Cut to Vertex Separator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Claim:** Every finite-capacity $s$–$t$ cut in $N(G)$ corresponds to a vertex separator in $G$, and conversely.

**Proof (Exhaustive):**

Let $(S, T)$ be a minimum $s_{\text{out}}$–$t_{\text{in}}$ cut in $N(G)$ with $c(S, T) = k$.

**Observation 1 — No split edge can be in $S \to T$ direction in a minimum cut:**
The split edge $(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}})$ has capacity **1**. If a path crosses from $v_{\text{in}}$ to $v_{\text{out}}$, the cut uses capacity **1**. We can always find a min cut where for each split edge, either BOTH endpoints are in $S$ or BOTH are in $T$ (otherwise we can move the in-node to $T$ without increasing cut capacity).

**Observation 2 — Separator construction:**
Define

$$
X \;=\; \big\{ v \in V \setminus \{s, t\} \;\big|\; v_{\text{in}} \in S \text{ and } v_{\text{out}} \in T \big\}
$$

Then $X$ is a vertex separator of $G$ separating $s$ from $t$, with

$$
\vert X \vert \;=\; c(S, T) \;=\; k
$$

**Conversely**, given a separator $X$ of size $k$ in $G$, define

$$
S \;=\; \big\{ v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}} \;\big|\; v \text{ is reachable from } s \text{ in } G - X \big\} \cup \{s_{\text{in}}, s_{\text{out}}\}
$$

and $T = V' \setminus S$. Then $(S, T)$ is an $s_{\text{out}}$–$t_{\text{in}}$ cut in $N(G)$ with $c(S, T) = \vert X \vert = k$.

### 3.4 Theorem — Menger's via Max-Flow Min-Cut

**Putting Lemmas 1 and 2 together:**

$$
\lambda_{s,t}(G) \;\stackrel{\text{Lemma 1}}{=}\; \max \text{ flow in } N(G) \;\stackrel{\text{Max-Flow Min-Cut}}{=}\; \min \text{ cut in } N(G) \;\stackrel{\text{Lemma 2}}{=}\; \kappa_{s,t}(G)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda_{s,t}(G) \;=\; \kappa_{s,t}(G)\;}
$$

### 3.5 Detailed Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Original Graph $G$:** Vertices $V = \{s, a, b, c, t\}$; Edges
$$
E \;=\; \big\{ \{s,a\}, \{s,b\}, \{a,c\}, \{b,c\}, \{a,t\}, \{b,t\} \big\}
$$

**Step 1 — Build $N(G)$ with capacities 1:**

| Directed Arc | Capacity |
|---|---|
| $(s_{\text{in}}, s_{\text{out}})$ | 1 |
| $(a_{\text{in}}, a_{\text{out}})$ | 1 |
| $(b_{\text{in}}, b_{\text{out}})$ | 1 |
| $(c_{\text{in}}, c_{\text{out}})$ | 1 |
| $(t_{\text{in}}, t_{\text{out}})$ | 1 |
| $(s_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$, $(a_{\text{out}}, s_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |
| $(s_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$, $(b_{\text{out}}, s_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |
| $(a_{\text{out}}, c_{\text{in}})$, $(c_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |
| $(b_{\text{out}}, c_{\text{in}})$, $(c_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |
| $(a_{\text{out}}, t_{\text{in}})$, $(t_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |
| $(b_{\text{out}}, t_{\text{in}})$, $(t_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$ | 1 |

**Step 2 — Max-Flow Computation:**

Two vertex-disjoint paths exist:
- $P_1$: $s \to a \to t$
- $P_2$: $s \to b \to t$

Therefore $\lambda_{s,t}(G) = 2$ and max flow value = 2.

**Step 3 — Min-Cut Verification:**

The cut $S = \{s_{\text{in}}, s_{\text{out}}\}$ vs. $T = $ rest has capacity = 2 (edges $(s_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$ and $(s_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$). No cut of capacity 1 exists because $s$ has degree 2 in $G$.

Therefore $\kappa_{s,t}(G) = 2$, confirming Menger's Theorem.

### 3.6 Analytic Matrix for KTU Board Answers

| Component | In Flow Network $N(G)$ | In Original Graph $G$ |
|---|---|---|
| Max flow value $k$ | Integer flow magnitude | # vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths |
| Min cut capacity $k$ | Sum of split-edges crossed | # vertices in min separator |
| Capacity of split edge | $c(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) = 1$ | Enforces single-use of vertex |
| Capacity of inter-vertex arc | $c(u_{\text{out}}, v_{\text{in}}) = 1$ | Enforces no parallel arcs |
| Source / Sink | $s_{\text{out}}$ / $t_{\text{in}}$ | $s$ / $t$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Undirected Graph G"] --> stageA["Stage A: Vertex Splitting"]
    stageA --> stageB["Stage B: Edge Orientation"]
    stageB --> stageC["Stage C: Capacity Assignment equal 1"]
    stageC --> stageD["Stage D: Directed Flow Network N of G"]
    stageD --> engine["Max Flow Algorithm e.g. Edmonds Karp Dinic"]
    engine --> outA["Output A: Max Flow Value k"]
    engine --> outB["Output B: Min S T Cut of capacity k"]
    outA --> mapA["Lemma 1: Flow to Vertex Disjoint Paths"]
    outB --> mapB["Lemma 2: Cut to Vertex Separator"]
    mapA --> final["Menger Equality lambda = kappa"]
    mapB --> final
```

### 4.3 Architecture of the Menger's Proof Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    blockB --> blockC["Apply Integrality Theorem for integer max flow"]
    blockC --> blockD["Establish Bijection: Flow to Disjoint Paths"]
    blockD --> blockE["Establish Bijection: Cut to Vertex Separator"]
    blockE --> blockF["Combine: max flow equals min cut implies lambda equals kappa"]
    blockF --> blockG["Conclusion: Menger Theorem Proven"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the vertex version of Menger's Theorem. Define the terms "internally vertex-disjoint paths" and "vertex separator" used in the theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Menger's Theorem (Vertex Version):** Let $G = (V, E)$ be a finite undirected connected graph and $s, t \in V$ be two non-adjacent vertices. The maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths equals the minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects $s$ from $t$.
>
> **Internally vertex-disjoint paths:** A collection of $s$–$t$ paths such that no two paths share any vertex other than $s$ and $t$ themselves.
>
> **Vertex separator:** A set $X \subseteq V \setminus \{s, t\}$ such that every $s$–$t$ path in $G$ contains at least one vertex of $X$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem. What is the "integrality property" that the proof of Menger's Theorem relies upon?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:** In any flow network with integer capacities, the value of the maximum $s$–$t$ flow equals the capacity of the minimum $s$–$t$ cut.
>
> **Integrality Property:** If all edge capacities in a flow network are integers, then there exists a maximum flow in which the flow on every edge is an **integer**. The proof of Menger's Theorem requires integer flows to extract **discrete paths** (each unit of flow corresponds to one path). **(1 Mark for statement, 1 Mark for integrality role, 1 Mark for implication).**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]*

**(a)** State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem. Define the auxiliary flow network $N(G)$ constructed from an undirected graph $G$ for proving Menger's Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem, prove Menger's Theorem (vertex version) for a finite undirected graph $G$ between two non-adjacent vertices $s$ and $t$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**

- **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (3 Marks):** In any single-source single-sink network with integer capacities $c : E \to \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, the value of the maximum $s$–$t$ flow $f$ equals the capacity of the minimum $s$–$t$ cut:
$$\max \vert f \vert \;=\; \min_{(S,T)} c(S, T)$$

- **Auxiliary Network Construction (4 Marks):** Given undirected $G = (V, E)$ with non-adjacent $s, t$:
  - Replace each $v \in V$ with $v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}$ and connect with edge of capacity **1**.
  - Replace each $\{u, v\} \in E$ with two directed arcs $(u_{\text{out}}, v_{\text{in}})$ and $(v_{\text{out}}, u_{\text{in}})$, each of capacity **1**.
  - Declare $s_{\text{out}}$ as source, $t_{\text{in}}$ as sink.

**(b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1: Show every integer $s_{\text{out}}$–$t_{\text{in}}$ flow of value $k$ in $N(G)$ gives $k$ vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths in $G$ (using capacity-1 split edges to enforce disjointness). **(3 Marks)**

Step 2: Show every $s_{\text{out}}$–$t_{\text{in}}$ cut of capacity $k$ in $N(G)$ gives a vertex separator of size $k$ in $G$ (by collecting vertices $v$ with $v_{\text{in}} \in S$ and $v_{\text{out}} \in T$). **(2 Marks)**

Step 3: By the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem, $\max$ flow $= \min$ cut, hence
$$\lambda_{s,t}(G) = \kappa_{s,t}(G)$$
which is Menger's Theorem. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]*

**(a)** Explain the **edge version** of Menger's Theorem. How does the construction of the auxiliary network differ for the edge version compared to the vertex version? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the graph $G$ with $V = \{s, a, b, c, t\}$ and $E = \{\{s,a\}, \{s,b\}, \{a,b\}, \{a,c\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,t\}\}$. Using the Max-Flow Min-Cut reduction, find:
  - (i) The maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths.
  - (ii) A minimum vertex separator.
  - (iii) Verify that the max-flow value equals the min-cut capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**

**Edge Version of Menger's Theorem:** The maximum number of **edge-disjoint** $s$–$t$ paths in $G$ equals the minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects $s$ from $t$.

**Difference in Construction:** For the edge version, **no vertex splitting is required**. Simply orient each undirected edge as a single directed arc (or two-way with capacity 1, depending on convention) of capacity **1** in the flow network. The vertex-splitting trick is specific to the **vertex version** because it forces each vertex to be used at most once. **(7 Marks for explanation + contrast table.)**

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**Construction of $N(G)$ with vertex splitting:**

| Edge in $G$ | Arcs in $N(G)$ with capacity 1 |
|---|---|
| $\{s, a\}$ | $(s_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$, $(a_{\text{out}}, s_{\text{in}})$ |
| $\{s, b\}$ | $(s_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$, $(b_{\text{out}}, s_{\text{in}})$ |
| $\{a, b\}$ | $(a_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$, $(b_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$ |
| $\{a, c\}$ | $(a_{\text{out}}, c_{\text{in}})$, $(c_{\text{out}}, a_{\text{in}})$ |
| $\{b, c\}$ | $(b_{\text{out}}, c_{\text{in}})$, $(c_{\text{out}}, b_{\text{in}})$ |
| $\{c, t\}$ | $(c_{\text{out}}, t_{\text{in}})$, $(t_{\text{out}}, c_{\text{in}})$ |

**(i) Max vertex-disjoint paths:** $P_1: s \to a \to c \to t$ and $P_2: s \to b \to c$ — but $c$ is shared! Better: $P_1: s \to a \to c \to t$ and $P_2: s \to b \to \text{??}$ — since $b$ connects only to $c$, only ONE vertex-disjoint path exists through $c$. However, $P_1: s \to a \to c \to t$, $P_2: s \to b \to a \to c$ shares $a$. So **maximum vertex-disjoint paths = 1**. Wait — actually $c$ has degree 2, $a$ has degree 3. Re-examination: $P_1: s \to a \to c \to t$ is the only path. $\boxed{\lambda_{s,t}(G) = 1}$.

**(ii) Minimum vertex separator:** The set $\{c\}$ alone disconnects $s$ from $t$. So $\kappa_{s,t}(G) = 1$.

**(iii) Verification:** Max flow = 1, Min cut = 1 (the cut isolating $c_{\text{in}}$ or $c_{\text{out}}$ from the rest). $\lambda = \kappa = 1$. **Menger's Theorem verified.** **(7 Marks: 2 + 2 + 3 for parts (i), (ii), (iii).)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the source/sink pair:** In the auxiliary network, the source is $s_{\text{out}}$ and the sink is $t_{\text{in}}$ (NOT $s_{\text{in}}$ and $t_{\text{out}}$) — losing **2 marks** for the wrong choice.
> 2. **Setting capacity > 1 for split edges:** If a student sets $c(v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}) = \infty$ or $2$, the disjointness property collapses — full **3-mark** penalty in Part B.
> 3. **Skipping the integrality theorem mention:** KTU examiners explicitly look for "**integrality of max flow**" — this is a 1–2 mark item often overlooked.
> 4. **Confusing vertex vs edge version:** The construction is DIFFERENT. Mixing them up loses **3–4 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Not showing the bijection both ways:** The proof needs both Lemma 1 (flow → paths) AND Lemma 2 (cut → separator). Skipping one direction costs **3 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Menger's Theorem (Vertex):** $\lambda_{s,t}(G) = \kappa_{s,t}(G)$ — max vertex-disjoint $s$–$t$ paths = min vertex separator.
- **Menger's Theorem (Edge):** $\lambda'_{s,t}(G) = \kappa'_{s,t}(G)$ — max edge-disjoint paths = min edge cut.
- **Max-Flow Min-Cut:** $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T)$ — the workhorse theorem.
- **Construction keys:** Split every vertex into $v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{out}}$ joined by a unit-capacity edge; orient every original edge as two unit-capacity arcs.
- **Integrality Theorem:** Integer capacities ⇒ integer max flow ⇒ each unit of flow = one disjoint path.
- **Source = $s_{\text{out}}$, Sink = $t_{\text{in}}$** — memorize this convention for KTU board exams.
- **Bijection must be both directions:** flow↔paths AND cut↔separator. One-way proof is incomplete.
- **Ford–Fulkerson algorithm** can be used to compute the max flow in $N(G)$ and hence find both $\lambda$ and $\kappa$.
- **Time complexity:** With Edmonds–Karp, the reduction yields a polynomial-time algorithm for vertex connectivity.
- **Don't confuse:** "min cut" in flow = sum of split-edge capacities = number of separator vertices (NOT number of edges).
- **KTU frequently asked:** Prove Menger's Theorem using Max-Flow Min-Cut (14-mark standard question).
- **Cross-topic link:** The same reduction technique is used in **Menger's Theorem**, **König's Theorem** (bipartite matching), and **Hall's Marriage Theorem**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertex Coloring and Upper Bounds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vertex Coloring and Upper Bounds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Vertex Coloring

A **vertex coloring** (or simply **coloring**) of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is an assignment of colors to each vertex of $G$ such that no two adjacent vertices (vertices joined by an edge) receive the same color. Formally, it is a function

$$c : V(G) \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$$

such that for every edge $uv \in E(G)$, we have $c(u) \neq c(v)$.

A coloring that uses at most $k$ distinct colors is called a **proper $k$-coloring** of $G$. The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ is the smallest integer $k$ for which a proper $k$-coloring of $G$ exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for KTU 2024:**
> - $\chi(G)$ — **chromatic number** of $G$ (minimum colors required).
> - $k$-colorable — graph admits a proper coloring with $\leq k$ colors.
> - $\Delta(G)$ — **maximum degree** of any vertex in $G$.
> - $\delta(G)$ — **minimum degree** in $G$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Map Coloring Analogy:** Imagine the districts of Kerala as vertices of a graph, with an edge between two districts whenever they share a common border. The famous "Four Color Theorem" guarantees that we can color every such map using at most **4 colors** so that no two bordering districts share the same color. This is the *chromatic number of the underlying planar graph.*

**Other Engineering Analogy — Register Allocation in Compilers:**
Every CPU has a limited number of **hardware registers** (think of them as colors). Variables being computed at the same time are linked by an edge in an *interference graph*. Coloring this graph with $k$ colors tells the compiler that **$k$ registers are sufficient** to hold all simultaneously-live variables without conflict.

## 1.3 Elementary Examples

| Graph Family | Chromatic Number $\chi(G)$ | Reason |
|--------------|----------------------------|--------|
| Empty graph $\overline{K_n}$ | $1$ | No adjacent vertices at all. |
| Complete graph $K_n$ | $n$ | Every pair of vertices is adjacent. |
| Even cycle $C_{2k}$ | $2$ | Bipartite — alternate two colors. |
| Odd cycle $C_{2k+1}$ | $3$ | Cannot be bipartite. |
| Bipartite graph | $2$ | Two color classes (when not empty). |
| Petersen graph | $3$ | Famous non-Hamiltonian example. |
| Path / Tree | $2$ | Both are bipartite. |

## 1.4 Why "Upper Bounds" Matter

In most real engineering settings (VLSI design, frequency assignment, exam timetabling) we cannot afford to *find* the exact chromatic number — the problem is **NP-hard** in general. Hence, we estimate it from above using tractable graph parameters such as the **maximum degree $\Delta(G)$**. An *upper bound* on $\chi(G)$ is a value $U$ such that $\chi(G) \leq U$; it certifies a feasible number of resources/colors needed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of Upper-Bounding the Chromatic Number

The standard analytical ladder for bounding $\chi(G)$ from above is:

1. **Choose an ordering of vertices** $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$ (any order is admissible).
2. **Color greedily** — when we reach $v_i$, assign the *smallest positive integer* not yet used by any already-colored neighbor of $v_i$.
3. **Count the worst case** — at the moment we color $v_i$, at most $\deg(v_i)$ neighbors have already received colors (all neighbors earlier in the order), so we never need more than $\deg(v_i) + 1$ colors for that vertex.
4. **Take the maximum** over all vertices: total colors $\leq \max_i(\deg(v_i) + 1) = \Delta(G) + 1$.

This gives the **trivial / greedy upper bound** $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$.

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Results

### Theorem 1 — Trivial (Greedy) Bound
For every simple graph $G$:

$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$$

### Theorem 2 — Brooks' Theorem (1941)
If $G$ is a connected simple graph that is **neither a complete graph** $K_n$ and **nor an odd cycle**, then:

$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$$

This is a strict tightening of the trivial bound by one unit in almost all non-trivial cases.

### Theorem 3 — Heawood's Six-Color Theorem (1890)
Every **planar graph** $G$ satisfies:

$$\chi(G) \leq 6$$

### Theorem 4 — Five-Color Theorem (Heawood, 1890)
Every planar graph $G$ satisfies:

$$\chi(G) \leq 5$$

### Theorem 5 — Four-Color Theorem (Appel & Haken, 1976)
Every planar graph $G$ satisfies:

$$\chi(G) \leq 4$$

### Theorem 6 — Szekeres–Wilf Bound (1968)

$$\chi(G) \leq \max_{H \subseteq G} \big(\delta(H) + 1\big)$$

where $H$ ranges over all *induced subgraphs* of $G$. This is often sharper than $\Delta(G)+1$ for sparse regions.

### Theorem 7 — Clique Lower Bound
For any clique $K_m \subseteq G$:

$$\chi(G) \geq m \quad \text{in particular} \quad \chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$$

where $\omega(G)$ is the **clique number**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula compendium** for KTU Module 3 problems. **Note:** All absolute-value / cardinality notations use `\vert` to prevent breaking the table syntax.

| **#** | **Result / Formula** | **Statement** | **Tightness** |
|------:|----------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ | Trivial greedy bound | Tight for $K_n$ and odd cycles. |
| 2 | $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$ | Brooks' theorem (connected, not $K_n$, not odd cycle) | Tight for even cycles, complete bipartite. |
| 3 | $\chi(G) \leq 6$ | Heawood (planar) | Tight for $K_6$-subgraph-free planar. |
| 4 | $\chi(G) \leq 5$ | Five-Color (planar) | Tighter than Heawood. |
| 5 | $\chi(G) \leq 4$ | Four-Color (planar) | Tight — infinite planar 4-chromatic families. |
| 6 | $\chi(G) \leq \max_H(\delta(H)+1)$ | Szekeres–Wilf | Sub-linear in $\Delta$ for many graphs. |
| 7 | $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ | Clique lower bound | Equality for perfect graphs. |
| 8 | $\chi(G) \geq \lceil n / \alpha(G) \rceil$ | Independent set bound | $\alpha(G)$ = independence number. |
| 9 | $\chi(G) \cdot \chi(\overline{G}) \geq n$ | Nordhaus–Gaddum | Holds for all $G$ on $n$ vertices. |
| 10 | $\chi(G) \leq 1 + \max_{i} \delta(G_i)$ | degeneracy bound | $G_i$ = subgraph after removing min-degree vertex. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these bounds are used in industry:**
> - **Compiler optimization** — chromatic number of the *interference graph* equals the minimum number of machine registers.
> - **Mobile network frequency planning** — adjacent cells must transmit on different channels; $\chi$ = number of channels.
> - **Exam timetabling at KTU** — courses sharing students are "adjacent"; $\chi$ = minimum exam slots.
> - **VLSI channel routing** — wires crossing the same channel = same color, minimizing channels.
> - **Sudoku puzzles** — Latin-square interpretation is a 9-coloring problem on a $9 \times 9$ graph.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Trivial Bound $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$

We prove the bound constructively via the **greedy coloring algorithm**. Let $G = (V,E)$ be any simple graph on $n$ vertices, and let $\Delta(G) = \max_{v \in V} \deg(v)$.

**Step 1 — Fix an arbitrary ordering.**
Choose any ordering of the vertices, for instance

$$v_1, \; v_2, \; v_3, \; \dots, \; v_n$$

**Step 2 — Initialize the color palette.**
Maintain a palette $C = \{1, 2, 3, \dots\}$ of positive integers.

**Step 3 — Iterative coloring rule.**
For each $i$ from $1$ to $n$:

$$c(v_i) \;=\; \min\big\{\, k \in \mathbb{N} \;\big\vert\; k \notin \{\, c(v_j) : v_j \in N(v_i) \text{ and } j < i \,\} \,\big\}$$

That is, $v_i$ receives the smallest color not yet used by any *earlier* neighbor.

**Step 4 — Count the worst case for $v_i$.**
At the moment $v_i$ is colored, at most $\deg(v_i)$ of its neighbors have been processed (those earlier in the order). The remaining neighbors, if any, will be colored later and pose no constraint on $v_i$ right now. Therefore the set of forbidden colors has size $\leq \deg(v_i)$, so

$$c(v_i) \;\leq\; \deg(v_i) + 1 \;\leq\; \Delta(G) + 1$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate over all vertices.**
Since the bound holds for *every* $v_i$, the global number of distinct colors used is at most

$$\max_{1 \leq i \leq n} c(v_i) \;\leq\; \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \big(\deg(v_i) + 1\big) \;\leq\; \Delta(G) + 1$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
A proper coloring of $G$ using at most $\Delta(G) + 1$ colors has been produced. By definition of $\chi(G)$ as the *minimum* number of colors in any proper coloring, we have

$$\chi(G) \;\leq\; \Delta(G) + 1 \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Brooks' Bound for the Non-Complete, Non-Odd-Cycle Case

We state Brooks' theorem: *If $G$ is a connected simple graph which is neither a complete graph nor an odd cycle, then $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$.*

**Proof Sketch (Constructive Ordering Argument):**

**Step 1 — Bounded-degree setup.**
Since $G$ is not a complete graph and not an odd cycle, $G$ has at least one vertex of degree $\leq \Delta(G) - 1$ unless $G$ is a regular graph of degree $\Delta$. We handle the two cases separately.

**Step 2 — Case A: $G$ has a vertex $v$ with $\deg(v) \leq \Delta(G) - 1$.**
Order the vertices so that $v_n = v$ (i.e., put $v$ *last* in the order). Recolor greedily. At every step $i < n$, vertex $v_i$ has at most $\deg(v_i) \leq \Delta(G)$ earlier neighbors, so it uses a color from $\{1, 2, \dots, \Delta(G)\}$. For $v_n = v$, its neighbors $\{v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}\}$ may collectively use *all* $\Delta(G)$ colors. But $\deg(v) \leq \Delta(G) - 1$, so at least one color from $\{1, \dots, \Delta(G)\}$ remains free for $v$. Hence the greedy algorithm succeeds with $\Delta(G)$ colors.

**Step 3 — Case B: $G$ is $\Delta$-regular.**
Since $G$ is not a complete graph and not an odd cycle, $G$ has at least one connected component that contains neither a single edge nor a triangle as a spanning component at the very end. We pick two non-adjacent vertices $u, w$ that lie on a common path. We perform a **block decomposition** and run the greedy algorithm on a sequence of subgraphs, each with at least one vertex of degree $< \Delta(G)$, which lets us reuse one of the $\Delta(G)$ colors.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
In both cases, a proper $\Delta(G)$-coloring exists. Therefore

$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Heawood's Six-Color Bound for Planar Graphs

We show $\chi(G) \leq 6$ for any planar graph $G$.

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's formula.**
A connected planar simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices and $e$ edges satisfies

$$n - e + f \;=\; 2$$

where $f$ is the number of faces.

**Step 2 — Bound edges by face constraints.**
Each face has at least $3$ boundary edges, and each edge borders at most $2$ faces. Therefore

$$2e \;\geq\; 3f \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f \;\leq\; \frac{2e}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Euler's formula.**

$$2 \;=\; n - e + f \;\leq\; n - e + \frac{2e}{3} \;=\; n - \frac{e}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $e$.**

$$e \;\leq\; 3n - 6$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Handshaking Lemma.**

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) \;=\; 2e \;\leq\; 6n - 12$$

**Step 6 — Pigeonhole on average degree.**

$$\frac{1}{n} \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) \;\leq\; 6 - \frac{12}{n} \;<\; 6$$

So the *average* degree is strictly less than $6$, and consequently there exists at least one vertex $v$ with $\deg(v) \leq 5$.

**Step 7 — Inductive coloring.**
By induction on $n$. The base case $n \leq 6$ is trivial (assign each vertex a distinct color from $\{1,\dots,6\}$). For the inductive step, remove a vertex $v$ of degree $\leq 5$. The remaining graph $G - v$ is still planar, hence by induction it admits a proper $6$-coloring. The at-most-$5$ neighbors of $v$ collectively forbid at most $5$ colors, leaving at least one color from $\{1,\dots,6\}$ available for $v$. Restore $v$ with that color. Hence

$$\chi(G) \;\leq\; 6 \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Coloring the Petersen Graph

> [!NOTE]
> **Petersen Graph $P$:** $n = 10$ vertices, $3$-regular, so $\Delta = 3$. The trivial bound gives $\chi(P) \leq 4$, while Brooks' theorem tightens this to $\chi(P) \leq 3$. The Petersen graph is in fact **$3$-colorable** but not $2$-colorable (it contains an odd cycle $C_5$).

**Coloring construction (3 colors: R, G, B):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer 5-cycle:} \;& v_1 = R,\; v_2 = G,\; v_3 = R,\; v_4 = G,\; v_5 = B \\
\text{Inner 5-star:} \;& u_1 = G,\; u_2 = B,\; u_3 = G,\; u_4 = R,\; u_5 = B
\end{aligned}
$$

One verifies that every edge of $P$ has endpoints with distinct colors. Therefore $\chi(P) = 3$, which matches the lower bound $\chi(P) \geq \omega(P) = 2$ combined with the non-bipartite nature $\chi(P) \geq 3$.

## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Dependencies

$$\boxed{\;\omega(G) \;\leq\; \chi(G) \;\leq\; \max_H (\delta(H)+1) \;\leq\; \Delta(G) + 1\;}$$

with the left inequality tight for perfect graphs, and the right inequality tightest (in expectation) for the Szekeres–Wilf bound rather than the naive $\Delta(G)+1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Greedy Vertex Coloring

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **operational flow** of the greedy vertex-coloring algorithm. Node identifiers are kept strictly alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid parser.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Graph G] --> A1[Compute DeltaG = max degree]
    A1 --> A2[Order vertices v1 to vn]
    A2 --> A3[Initialize color array C of size n]
    A3 --> A4[Set i = 1]
    A4 --> A5{Is i greater than n?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Return color array C]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Collect neighbors N of vi already colored]
    A7 --> A8[Form forbidden set F from colors of N]
    A8 --> A9[Find smallest positive integer k not in F]
    A9 --> A10[Assign C vi = k]
    A10 --> A11[Increment i]
    A11 --> A5
    A6 --> A12[End: Proper Coloring of G]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Upper-Bound Hierarchy for Chromatic Number

The diagram below shows the **inclusion hierarchy** of common upper bounds, from tightest to loosest.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Exact chromatic number chi G] --> B1[Brooks bound: chi G le Delta G]
    B0 --> B2[Szekeres Wilf bound]
    B1 --> B3[Trivial greedy bound: chi G le Delta G plus 1]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Planar Heawood bound: chi G le 6]
    B3 --> B5[Planar Five Color bound: chi G le 5]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Planar Four Color bound: chi G le 4]
    B6 --> B7[Universal trivial bound: chi G le n]
```

## 4.3 Visual Mermaid: Sample 3-Coloring of $C_5$

A worked illustration of a **3-coloring of the odd cycle $C_5$**, demonstrating that an odd cycle cannot be 2-colored.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[1: Red] --- n2[2: Green]
    n2 --- n3[3: Red]
    n3 --- n4[4: Green]
    n4 --- n5[5: Blue]
    n5 --- n1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The vertices labelled 1 through 5 are connected in a cycle. Vertex 5 is forced to use a *third* color (Blue) because its neighbors, vertices 4 and 1, already use Green and Red respectively. This is the canonical proof that $\chi(C_{2k+1}) = 3$.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Decision Logic for Bound Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Given Graph G] --> D1{Is G planar?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Apply Four Color Theorem: chi G le 4]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Is G complete or odd cycle?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Use exact value: chi G = n or 3]
    D3 -- No --> D5{Is G connected?}
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Apply Brooks Theorem: chi G le Delta G]
    D5 -- No --> D7[Apply Brooks on each component]
    D2 --> D8[Done]
    D4 --> D8
    D6 --> D8
    D7 --> D8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Proper $k$-coloring of a graph.
(b) Chromatic number $\chi(G)$.

**Model Answer:**

(a) A **proper $k$-coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a function $c : V \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ such that for every edge $uv \in E(G)$, $c(u) \neq c(v)$. In other words, adjacent vertices receive different colors.
*Example:* $C_4$ admits a proper 2-coloring: alternate vertices Red and Blue.

(b) The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ of a graph $G$ is the smallest positive integer $k$ for which $G$ admits a proper $k$-coloring. Formally, $\chi(G) = \min \{ k : G \text{ is } k\text{-colorable} \}$.
*Example:* $\chi(K_5) = 5$ because every pair of vertices in $K_5$ is adjacent, requiring distinct colors.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Brooks' theorem and mention one graph family for which the bound is tight.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brooks' Theorem (1941):** If $G$ is a connected simple graph that is neither a complete graph nor an odd cycle, then $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$.

The bound is **tight** for even cycles $C_{2k}$ and complete bipartite graphs $K_{m,n}$ when $m, n \geq 2$ — both are 2-colorable while $\Delta(K_{m,n}) = \max(m, n)$ which can be arbitrarily large. It is also tight for complete graphs in the sense that Brooks' theorem does *not* apply to them (they require $n$ colors).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Prove that for any simple graph $G$, $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$, by describing and analysing the greedy coloring algorithm.

**(b)** [7 Marks] State and prove Brooks' theorem for connected graphs that are not complete graphs and not odd cycles.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Algorithm description:** Order vertices arbitrarily as $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$. For $i = 1$ to $n$, assign to $v_i$ the smallest positive integer $c(v_i) \in \{1, 2, \dots\}$ that has not been assigned to any neighbor of $v_i$ already colored (i.e., any $v_j$ with $j < i$ and $v_j v_i \in E$).

**Correctness:** By construction, when $v_i$ is colored, none of its already-colored neighbors share its color. Vertices colored later pose no immediate constraint. Hence the final coloring is proper.

**Number of colors used:** At step $i$, the number of forbidden colors equals the number of already-colored neighbors of $v_i$, which is at most $\deg(v_i)$. So

$$c(v_i) \;\leq\; \deg(v_i) + 1 \;\leq\; \Delta(G) + 1$$

The maximum over all $i$ gives the global bound, hence $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)
