**(a)** Define a planar graph and a region (face). For a connected planar graph with 8 vertices, each of degree 3, determine the number of regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove Kuratowski's theorem. Show that $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Definition:** A graph is **planar** if it can be drawn in the plane with no edge crossings. A **face** (region) is a maximal portion of the plane such that any two points in it can be connected by a curve that does not intersect the graph.

**Given:** Connected planar graph, $V = 8$, every vertex has degree $3$ (cubic graph).

**Step 1 — Apply Handshaking Lemma to find $E$.**

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2E \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 8 \times 3 = 2E \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E = 12
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's formula to find $F$.**

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

$$
8 - 12 + F = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F = 6
$$

So the graph has $\boxed{6}$ faces (regions). $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Kuratowski's Theorem (1930):** A graph $G$ is planar if and only if $G$ does **not** contain a subgraph that is a **subdivision** of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.

**Proof Sketch (necessity):** If $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a subgraph, then since subdivisions preserve non-planarity (the added degree-2 vertices cannot fix the crossings of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$), $G$ must be non-planar. (Sufficiency is harder and was proven in 1930.)

**Showing $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar:**

**Step 1:** $K_{3,3}$ is the complete bipartite graph with parts of size 3.

$$
V = 6, \quad E = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

**Step 2:** $K_{3,3}$ is bipartite and simple, so if it were planar, the bipartite bound $E \leq 2V - 4$ would hold:

$$
2V - 4 = 2(6) - 4 = 8
$$

**Step 3:** But $E = 9 > 8$, a contradiction. Hence $K_{3,3}$ is **non-planar**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a planar graph. Prove that in any connected planar graph with $V \geq 3$, we have $E \leq 3V - 6$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A connected planar graph has 20 edges. If the graph is bipartite, what is the maximum number of vertices it can have? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Planar Graph Definition:** A graph is planar if it can be drawn in the plane such that no two edges cross except at shared endpoints.

**Proof of $E \leq 3V - 6$ for simple connected planar graph with $V \geq 3$:**

**Step 1 — Let $F_i$ denote the number of faces with exactly $i$ edges on the boundary.** Since $G$ is simple, every face has at least 3 edges on its boundary. So $F_1 = F_2 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Count edge-face incidences.** Each edge borders exactly 2 faces, hence:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{F} \text{edges on face}_i = 2E
$$

**Step 3 — Lower bound the sum.** Since each face has $\geq 3$ edges:

$$
2E = \sum_{i=1}^{F} (\text{edges on face}_i) \geq 3F \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F \leq \frac{2E}{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's formula.**

$$
V - E + F = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F = 2 - V + E
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the inequalities.**

$$
2 - V + E \leq \frac{2E}{3}
$$

Multiply by 3:

$$
6 - 3V + 3E \leq 2E
$$

Rearrange:

$$
6 - 3V \leq 2E - 3E = -E
$$

$$
E \leq 3V - 6
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** Connected bipartite planar graph with $E = 20$.

**Formula:** For a connected bipartite planar simple graph with $V \geq 3$:

$$
E \leq 2V - 4
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the bound.**

$$
20 \leq 2V - 4
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V$.**

$$
24 \leq 2V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V \leq 12
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** The maximum number of vertices is $\boxed{12}$.

**Step 4 — Verification.** At $V = 12$, $E = 20$, check Euler: $F = 2 - V + E = 2 - 12 + 20 = 10$. Each face must have $\geq 4$ edges (bipartite ⇒ no triangles). Sum of face degrees: $4F = 40 = 2E$. ✓

$\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Top 5 Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the outer face:** When counting $F$ in Euler's formula, ALWAYS include the unbounded (outer) face. K9 students often forget this and lose 2-3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong bound:** Applying $E \leq 3V - 6$ to a *bipartite* graph. For bipartite graphs, use the **tighter** bound $E \leq 2V - 4$ to get full credit.
>
> 3. **Confusing "subdivision" with "minor":** A subdivision inserts degree-2 vertices; a minor may contract edges. Kuratowski uses **subdivisions**; Wagner uses **minors**. Don't mix them up.
>
> 4. **Not checking graph connectivity:** Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2$ holds only for **connected** graphs. For $k$ components, use $V - E + F = 1 + k$.
>
> 5. **Claiming $K_5$ is bipartite:** $K_5$ contains odd cycles (triangles), so it is **not** bipartite. The bipartite bound $E \leq 2V - 4$ cannot be applied to it. Use $E \leq 3V - 6$ instead (and that is enough to disprove planarity).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Planar Graphs (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Planar = drawable in a plane without edge crossings.
> ✅ **Euler's Formula:** $V - E + F = 2$ (connected case) / $V - E + F = 1 + k$ (general).
> ✅ **Edge Bounds:**
>    - Simple planar: $E \leq 3V - 6$
>    - Simple bipartite planar: $E \leq 2V - 4$
>    - Triangle-free planar: $E \leq 2V - 4$
> ✅ **Minimum Vertex Degree:** Every planar graph has a vertex with $\deg(v) \leq 5$.
> ✅ **Face-Edge Identity:** $\sum \deg(\text{face}) = 2E$.
> ✅ **Kuratowski's Theorem:** Planar ⟺ no subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.
> ✅ **Wagner's Theorem:** Planar ⟺ no minor of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.
> ✅ **Four Color Theorem:** Every simple planar graph is 4-colorable.
> ✅ **Dual Graph:** $V^* = F$, $E^* = E$, $F^* = V$.
> ✅ **Classical Non-Planar Graphs:** $K_5$ (5 vertices, 10 edges) and $K_{3,3}$ (6 vertices, 9 edges).
> ✅ **Real-World Examples:** PCB layout, VLSI routing, map coloring, utility networks, railway design.
> ✅ **Subdivision Property:** Subdivision does not change planarity.
> ✅ **Homeomorphic Graphs:** Same planarity status; share a common "ancestor" graph under subdivision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization of Planar Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterization of Planar Graphs**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planar Graph (KTU 2024 Standard Definition)**
> A graph $G = (V, E)$ is said to be **planar** if it can be drawn in the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that its edges intersect only at their common endpoints (vertices). Such a drawing is called a **planar embedding** or **plane graph**.

If a graph is drawn without any edge crossings (other than at shared vertices), it is embedded in the plane. Two graphs are considered **equivalent** (from a planarity perspective) if they are **homeomorphic** — i.e., obtained from each other by subdividing edges with additional vertices of degree 2.

### Key Related Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Face (Region)** | A maximal region of the plane bounded by edges in a plane graph. The unbounded region is called the *outer face*. |
| **Plane Graph** | A specific embedding (drawing) of a planar graph in the plane. |
| **Non-Planar Graph** | A graph that has *no* planar embedding. |
| **Maximal Planar Graph** | A planar graph to which no edge can be added without losing planarity (every face is a triangle). |
| **Outerplanar Graph** | A planar graph that has an embedding where all vertices lie on the boundary of the outer face. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Analogy**
> Imagine you are an electrical engineer designing a **Printed Circuit Board (PCB)**. You have a set of components (vertices) that need to be connected by copper traces (edges). However, the traces are not allowed to cross each other because the board is a flat 2D surface.
>
> - If you **can** route all the wires without crossings → your circuit is a **planar graph**.
> - If you **cannot** avoid crossings (no matter how hard you try) → your circuit is **non-planar**, and you would need multiple layers of the PCB (a multi-layer board) or jumpers.

This is exactly the essence of planarity: **Can the graph be redrawn on a flat surface without edge crossings?**

### Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **VLSI Circuit Design**: Minimizing crossings in chip layouts.
- **Map Coloring**: The famous Four Color Theorem states that any planar map (planar graph) can be colored using only 4 colors such that no two adjacent regions share the same color.
- **Railway/Road Network Design**: Determining if a transportation network can be built without overpasses.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The **Four Color Theorem** (proved by Appel & Haken, 1976, using computer assistance) is a celebrated direct consequence of planar graph theory.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Euler's Constant for Planar Graphs**: $V - E + F = 2$ (for connected plane graphs).
- **Maximum Edge Density**: $E \leq 3V - 6$ for simple planar graphs with $V \geq 3$.
- **Bipartite Planar Bound**: $E \leq 2V - 4$ for simple bipartite planar graphs with $V \geq 3$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Euler's Formula — The Cornerstone Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Formula (1758)**
> For every **connected plane graph** $G$ with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces:
> $$V - E + F = 2$$

This is one of the most fundamental results in graph theory and topology. It relates the three primary structural components of any planar embedding.

### Why Does Euler's Formula Work?

- The formula is **topological** — it remains invariant regardless of how you deform the embedding.
- It is the **planar counterpart** of the Euler characteristic $\chi = 2$ for a sphere. (In fact, drawing a graph on a sphere is equivalent to drawing it on a plane via stereographic projection.)
- For a tree (connected, acyclic): $F = 1$, $E = V - 1$, so $V - (V-1) + 1 = 2$. ✓

## 2.2 Corollaries of Euler's Formula

### Corollary 1: Maximum Edges in a Simple Planar Graph

For any simple connected planar graph with $V \geq 3$:
$$E \leq 3V - 6$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- Every face is bounded by at least 3 edges (since there are no loops or multi-edges).
- Each edge is shared by exactly 2 faces.
- Summing face degrees: $2E \geq 3F \implies F \leq \frac{2E}{3}$.
- Substituting into Euler's formula: $V - E + \frac{2E}{3} \geq 2 \implies V - \frac{E}{3} \geq 2 \implies E \leq 3V - 6$.

### Corollary 2: Planar Bipartite Graph Bound

For any simple connected **bipartite** planar graph with $V \geq 3$:
$$E \leq 2V - 4$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- In a bipartite graph, every cycle has even length $\geq 4$.
- Each face is bounded by at least 4 edges.
- $2E \geq 4F \implies F \leq \frac{E}{2}$.
- Substituting: $V - E + \frac{E}{2} \geq 2 \implies V - \frac{E}{2} \geq 2 \implies E \leq 2V - 4$.

### Corollary 3: Triangle-Free Planar Bound

A triangle-free simple planar graph with $V \geq 3$ satisfies $E \leq 2V - 4$.

## 2.3 The Two Fundamental Non-Planar Graphs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kuratowski's Theorem (1930)**
> A graph $G$ is planar **if and only if** it does not contain a **subdivision** of either $K_5$ (Complete graph on 5 vertices) or $K_{3,3}$ (Complete bipartite graph) as a subgraph.

| Graph | Vertices ($V$) | Edges ($E$) | Test using $E \leq 3V-6$ | Result |
|-------|----------------|--------------|--------------------------|--------|
| $K_5$ | 5 | 10 | $10 \leq 3(5)-6 = 9$? **No!** | **Non-Planar** |
| $K_{3,3}$ | 6 | 9 | $9 \leq 3(6)-6 = 12$? Yes | Inconclusive (use bipartite bound) |
| $K_{3,3}$ (bipartite) | 6 | 9 | $9 \leq 2(6)-4 = 8$? **No!** | **Non-Planar** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Even if a graph satisfies $E \leq 3V - 6$, it does **NOT** automatically mean it is planar. The inequality is a *necessary* condition, not *sufficient*. Always verify via Kuratowski's theorem for borderline cases.

## 2.4 Wagner's Theorem (Equivalent Formulation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wagner's Theorem (1937)**
> A graph $G$ is planar **if and only if** it does not contain $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a **minor**.

This is closely related to Kuratowski's theorem, but uses the concept of **graph minors** (obtained by edge contractions and deletions) instead of subdivisions.

## 2.5 Fary's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fary's Theorem (1948)**
> Every simple planar graph can be drawn in the plane using only **straight line segments** for edges, with no crossings.

This is a surprising result — even though the *definition* of planarity allows curved edges, planarity can always be achieved with straight lines.

## 2.6 Whitney's Theorem (Planar Embedding Uniqueness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Whitney's Theorem (1932)**
> Every **3-connected** planar graph has a **unique planar embedding** on the sphere (up to homeomorphism of the plane).

This means the sequence of faces around each vertex is uniquely determined for 3-connected planar graphs. This is the theoretical foundation for many graph drawing algorithms.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Theorem / Formula | Statement | Condition |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Euler's Formula** | $V - E + F = 2$ | Connected plane graph |
| 2 | **General Euler** | $V - E + F = 1 + C$ | $C$ = number of connected components |
| 3 | **Edge Bound (Simple)** | $E \leq 3V - 6$ | Simple planar, $V \geq 3$ |
| 4 | **Edge Bound (Bipartite)** | $E \leq 2V - 4$ | Simple bipartite planar, $V \geq 3$ |
| 5 | **Triangle-Free Bound** | $E \leq 2V - 4$ | Triangle-free simple planar, $V \geq 3$ |
| 6 | **Kuratowski's Thm** | Planar $\iff$ no $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ subdivision | General |
| 7 | **Wagner's Thm** | Planar $\iff$ no $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor | General |
| 8 | **Fary's Thm** | Planar $\implies$ straight-line embedding | Simple planar |
| 9 | **Whitney's Thm** | Unique embedding on sphere | 3-connected planar |
| 10 | **Face Count** | $F = 1 + E - V$ | From Euler's formula |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **VLSI Design** | Layer assignment to minimize PCB layers |
| **Computer Networks** | Topology design without interference |
| **GIS / Cartography** | Map coloring optimization |
| **Bioinformatics** | RNA secondary structure prediction |
| **Algorithm Design** | Planar separator theorem (sublinear algorithms) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Edges in a Simple Planar Graph

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** For a simple connected planar graph $G$ with $V \geq 3$ vertices, $E \leq 3V - 6$.

### Step 1 — Setup
Let $G$ be a connected simple planar graph with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces in some plane embedding.

### Step 2 — Face-Edge Counting Inequality
Since $G$ is simple and has no loops or multiple edges, and since $V \geq 3$, every face in the planar embedding is bounded by **at least 3 edges**.

Define the **degree of a face** $f_i$ as the number of edges on its boundary. Then:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{F} \deg(f_i) \geq 3F$$

### Step 3 — Double Counting the Boundary
**Key Insight:** Every edge is shared by exactly **2** adjacent faces (one on each side), except for edges forming bridges (in which case it is counted twice in the same face's boundary). Thus, the sum of face degrees equals exactly $2E$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{F} \deg(f_i) = 2E$$

### Step 4 — Combine Inequalities
From Steps 2 and 3:
$$2E \geq 3F$$

Solving for $F$:
$$F \leq \frac{2E}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Euler's Formula
Euler's formula states:
$$V - E + F = 2$$

Substituting the upper bound on $F$:
$$V - E + \frac{2E}{3} \geq 2$$

### Step 6 — Algebraic Simplification
$$V - E + \frac{2E}{3} \geq 2$$

$$V - \frac{3E}{3} + \frac{2E}{3} \geq 2$$

$$V - \frac{E}{3} \geq 2$$

Multiplying both sides by 3:
$$3V - E \geq 6$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E \leq 3V - 6} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: $K_5$ is Non-Planar

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** The complete graph $K_5$ is non-planar.

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters
For $K_5$:
$$V = 5, \quad E = \binom{5}{2} = 10$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Planar Upper Bound
Apply Corollary 1 (simple planar bound):
$$E \leq 3V - 6 = 3(5) - 6 = 9$$

### Step 3 — Compare
$$E_{K_5} = 10 > 9 = 3V - 6$$

### Step 4 — Conclude
Since the necessary condition $E \leq 3V - 6$ is violated, $K_5$ **cannot be planar**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: $K_{3,3}$ is Non-Planar

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** The complete bipartite graph $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar.

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters
For $K_{3,3}$:
$$V = 6, \quad E = 3 \times 3 = 9$$

### Step 2 — Check Simple Planar Bound
$$E \leq 3V - 6 = 3(6) - 6 = 12$$
Since $9 \leq 12$, the simple bound is **satisfied** — inconclusive.

### Step 3 — Apply Bipartite Bound
$K_{3,3}$ is bipartite. Apply the bipartite bound:
$$E \leq 2V - 4 = 2(6) - 4 = 8$$

### Step 4 — Compare
$$E_{K_{3,3}} = 9 > 8 = 2V - 4$$

### Step 5 — Conclude
Since the bipartite necessary condition is violated, $K_{3,3}$ **cannot be planar**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Planarity Testing Workflow

| Step | Action | Tool Used | Decision |
|------|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Count $V$ and $E$ | Basic counting | — |
| 2 | Check connectivity | DFS / BFS | If disconnected, treat each component |
| 3 | Test $E \leq 3V - 6$ | Corollary 1 | If violated → **Non-Planar** (STOP) |
| 4 | Test bipartite & $E \leq 2V - 4$ | Corollary 2 | If violated → **Non-Planar** (STOP) |
| 5 | Test triangle-free & $E \leq 2V - 4$ | Corollary 3 | If violated → **Non-Planar** (STOP) |
| 6 | Search for $K_5$ / $K_{3,3}$ subdivision | Kuratowski's Thm | If found → **Non-Planar** |
| 7 | Search for $K_5$ / $K_{3,3}$ minor | Wagner's Thm | If found → **Non-Planar** |
| 8 | Try constructive embedding | Hopcroft-Tarjan / Boyer-Myrvold | If successful → **Planar** |

## 3.5 Worked Example: $K_{3,3}$ Edge Counting Detailed

For a complete bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$:
$$V = m + n, \quad E = m \cdot n$$

Planarity test:
$$m \cdot n \leq 2(m + n) - 4$$
$$m \cdot n \leq 2m + 2n - 4$$
$$mn - 2m - 2n + 4 \leq 0$$
$$(m - 2)(n - 2) \leq 0$$

For $(m-2)(n-2) \leq 0$, one of $(m-2)$ or $(n-2)$ must be non-positive.

This means $K_{m,n}$ is bipartite-planar only when:
- $m = 1$ (star graph — always planar), or
- $m = 2$ (any $n$ — a series of parallel edges in a 2-partition forms a tree-like structure), or
- $n = 1$, or $n = 2$ (symmetric).

For $K_{3,3}$: $(3-2)(3-2) = 1 > 0$ → **Non-Planar**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Planarity Characterization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Graph G] --> nodeB[Count V and E]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is G connected?}
    nodeC -->|No| nodeD[Process each component]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Euler to each]
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeF[E <= 3V - 6 ?]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeG[Non-Planar: STOP]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeH{Is G bipartite?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[E <= 2V - 4 ?]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Search K5 or K33 subdivision]
    nodeI -->|No| nodeG
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Found| nodeG
    nodeJ -->|Not found| nodeK[Constructive embedding test]
    nodeK -->|Success| nodeL[Planar: G is planar]
    nodeK -->|Failure| nodeG
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: K5 Non-Planarity Demonstration

```mermaid
graph TD
    v1[K5 Vertex 1] --- v2[K5 Vertex 2]
    v1 --- v3[K5 Vertex 3]
    v1 --- v4[K5 Vertex 4]
    v1 --- v5[K5 Vertex 5]
    v2 --- v3
    v2 --- v4
    v2 --- v5
    v3 --- v4
    v3 --- v5
    v4 --- v5
```

**Interpretation:** This Mermaid block conceptually represents $K_5$ where every vertex is connected to every other vertex. In a true planar embedding, the 10th edge would **always** cross at least one other edge, confirming non-planarity through the inequality $10 > 9 = 3(5) - 6$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: $K_{3,3}$ Bipartite Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    a1[Part A1] --- b1[Part B1]
    a1 --- b2[Part B2]
    a1 --- b3[Part B3]
    a2[Part A2] --- b1
    a2 --- b2
    a2 --- b3
    a3[Part A3] --- b1
    a3 --- b2
    a3 --- b3
```

**Interpretation:** $K_{3,3}$ has 3 vertices in partition $A$ and 3 in partition $B$, with every cross-partition edge present. This is the second Kuratowski obstruction.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Planar Graph Theorems Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Euler Formula V minus E plus F equals 2] --> rootB[Corollaries Edge Bounds]
    rootB --> rootC[Simple Planar E le 3V minus 6]
    rootB --> rootD[Bipartite Planar E le 2V minus 4]
    rootA --> rootE[Characterization Theorems]
    rootE --> rootF[Kuratowski No K5 or K33 subdivision]
    rootE --> rootG[Wagner No K5 or K33 minor]
    rootE --> rootH[Whitney Unique 3-connected embedding]
    rootA --> rootI[Drawing Theorems]
    rootI --> rootJ[Fary Straight line embedding]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Connected Plane Graph] --> nodeY[Count V vertices]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Count E edges]
    nodeX --> nodeW[Count F faces including outer face]
    nodeY --> nodeOut[Apply V minus E plus F equals 2]
    nodeZ --> nodeOut
    nodeW --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeResult[Verify Euler identity]
```

```mermaid
    inputNode[Input Graph G] --> c1[Component Decomposition]
    c1 --> c2[Biconnected Component Decomposition SPQR Tree]
    c2 --> c3[Planarity Test on Each Block]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a planar graph. State Euler's formula for a connected plane graph.**

**Model Answer:**

A graph $G = (V, E)$ is called **planar** if it can be drawn in a plane such that no two edges intersect except at their common endpoints.

**Euler's Formula:** For a connected plane graph with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces:
$$V - E + F = 2$$

**Example:** A tree with $V$ vertices has $E = V - 1$ and $F = 1$ (one face), giving $V - (V-1) + 1 = 2$. ✓

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Kuratowski's theorem. Name the two graphs involved in the theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

**Kuratowski's Theorem:** A graph is planar if and only if it does **not** contain a subdivision of either of the following two graphs:
1. **$K_5$** — The complete graph on 5 vertices (5 vertices, 10 edges).
2. **$K_{3,3}$** — The complete bipartite graph on $3+3$ vertices (6 vertices, 9 edges).

These two graphs are called **Kuratowski's forbidden graphs** or **Kuratowski obstructions**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove Euler's formula for a connected plane graph. Hence derive the bound $E \leq 3V - 6$ for simple planar graphs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Kuratowski's theorem or the edge bound, prove that $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are non-planar.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Statement:** For a connected plane graph with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces: $V - E + F = 2$.

**Proof by induction on the number of edges $E$:**

**Base Case ($E = 0$):** The graph is a single isolated vertex, so $V = 1$, $E = 0$, $F = 1$. Then $1 - 0 + 1 = 2$. ✓

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume formula holds for all connected graphs with fewer than $n$ edges.

**Inductive Step ($E = n$):** Consider a connected graph $G$ with $E = n$ edges.

**Case 1:** $G$ is a tree. Then $E = V - 1$ and $F = 1$. So $V - (V-1) + 1 = 2$. ✓

**Case 2:** $G$ contains a cycle. Remove one edge $e$ from a cycle. The new graph $G' = G - e$ is still connected and has:
- $V' = V$ vertices
- $E' = E - 1$ edges
- The two faces separated by $e$ now merge into one, so $F' = F - 1$ faces.

By the inductive hypothesis:
$$V' - E' + F' = 2$$
$$V - (E-1) + (F-1) = 2$$
$$V - E + 1 + F - 1 = 2$$
$$V - E + F = 2 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Derivation of $E \leq 3V - 6$:**

In a simple planar graph with $V \geq 3$, every face has at least 3 boundary edges, so:
$$\sum_{f \in \text{faces}} \deg(f) \geq 3F$$

Since each edge borders exactly 2 faces:
$$\sum_{f} \deg(f) = 2E \implies 2E \geq 3F \implies F \leq \frac{2E}{3}$$

Substituting into Euler's formula:
$$V - E + F = 2 \implies F = 2 - V + E$$

Therefore:
$$2 - V + E \leq \frac{2E}{3}$$
$$6 - 3V + 3E \leq 2E$$
$$E \leq 3V - 6 \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Proof that $K_5$ is non-planar:**

For $K_5$: $V = 5$, $E = \binom{5}{2} = 10$.

Planar bound: $E \leq 3V - 6 = 3(5) - 6 = 9$.

Since $10 > 9$, $K_5$ **violates** the necessary condition for planarity.

Therefore, $K_5$ is non-planar. $\blacksquare$

**Proof that $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar:**

For $K_{3,3}$: $V = 6$, $E = 3 \times 3 = 9$.

Simple bound: $E \leq 3V - 6 = 12$. Satisfied — inconclusive.

$K_{3,3}$ is bipartite, so apply bipartite bound: $E \leq 2V - 4 = 2(6) - 4 = 8$.

Since $9 > 8$, $K_{3,3}$ **violates** the bipartite necessary condition.

Therefore, $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often try to use the simple bound $E \leq 3V-6$ for $K_{3,3}$ and incorrectly conclude it might be planar. You **must** use the bipartite bound for bipartite graphs. The simple bound is **necessary but not sufficient** for planarity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of graph homeomorphism. State and explain Kuratowski's theorem with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the graph $G$ with $V = 8$ vertices and $E = 16$ edges, determine if $G$ can be planar. Justify using Euler's formula. Also identify whether $G$ could be a triangulation (maximal planar graph) of 8 vertices.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Graph Homeomorphism:** Two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ are said to be **homeomorphic** if they can both be obtained from the same graph $H$ by successively **subdividing edges** with vertices of degree 2 (or by the reverse operation of *smoothing* vertices of degree 2).

Formally, $G_1 \cong G_2$ if there exists a graph $H$ such that both $G_1$ and $G_2$ are subdivisions of $H$.

**Example:** Consider $G_1 = P_3$ (path on 3 vertices: $a-c-b$) and $G_2 = P_4$ (path on 4 vertices: $a-d-c-b$). Both are homeomorphic to the same underlying path of 2 edges.

**Kuratowski's Theorem:** A graph $G$ is planar **if and only if** it does not contain a **subdivision** of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a subgraph.

**Explanation:** This means planarity is preserved/removable by:
- Adding or removing degree-2 vertices on edges.
- The theorem identifies $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ as the **minimal non-planar graphs**.

**Example Application:** Consider the Petersen graph (10 vertices, 15 edges). It contains a subdivision of $K_{3,3}$, so by Kuratowski's theorem, it is **non-planar**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $V = 8$, $E = 16$.

**Step 1 — Test Simple Planar Bound:**
$$E \leq 3V - 6$$
$$16 \leq 3(8) - 6 = 18$$

The inequality is **satisfied** (16 ≤ 18). So $G$ is **not ruled out** by this bound.

**Step 2 — Test Bipartite Bound (Assume bipartite):**
$$E \leq 2V - 4 = 2(8) - 4 = 12$$

If $G$ is bipartite, $16 > 12$ would violate the bound. So $G$ is non-planar only if it is bipartite.

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's Formula to Find Faces:**
For a connected plane graph:
$$F = 2 - V + E = 2 - 8 + 16 = 10$$

So if $G$ is planar, it would have 10 faces.

**Step 4 — Triangulation Test:**
A **triangulation** (maximal planar graph) of 8 vertices satisfies:
$$E = 3V - 6 = 3(8) - 6 = 18$$

Since $E = 16 \neq 18$, $G$ is **not a maximal planar graph** (i.e., not a triangulation) of 8 vertices. It has $18 - 16 = 2$ fewer edges than a full triangulation.

**Conclusion:** $G$ with $V=8, E=16$ passes the simple necessary bound, so it **could be planar** (the bound is necessary but not sufficient). However, it is **definitely not a triangulation** of 8 vertices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students will conclude $G$ is non-planar simply because $16 \neq 18$ for a triangulation. The correct interpretation is: $G$ may still be planar (just not maximal). Do not confuse "not a triangulation" with "non-planar."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams**

- **Planar Graph Definition:** Drawable in plane without edge crossings (except at vertices). *[3 marks favorite]*

- **Euler's Formula:** $V - E + F = 2$ — must be memorized verbatim for connected plane graphs. *[Frequently asked in Part A]*

- **Simple Planar Edge Bound:** $E \leq 3V - 6$ for $V \geq 3$. **NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT** for planarity.

- **Bipartite Planar Edge Bound:** $E \leq 2V - 4$ for $V \geq 3$. Apply ONLY to bipartite graphs.

- **Kuratowski's Theorem:** Planar $\iff$ no subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$. **THE** characterization theorem.

- **Wagner's Theorem:** Planar $\iff$ no minor of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.

- **$K_5$ is non-planar:** $V=5, E=10 > 3V-6 = 9$.

- **$K_{3,3}$ is non-planar:** $V=6, E=9 > 2V-4 = 8$ (use bipartite bound, NOT simple bound).

- **Fary's Theorem:** Every simple planar graph has a straight-line embedding.

- **Whitney's Theorem:** Every 3-connected planar graph has a unique planar embedding on the sphere.

- **Face Count:** Always include the outer (unbounded) face. $F = 1 + E - V$ for connected plane graphs.

- **Forests and Trees:** Always planar. A tree on $V$ vertices has $E = V-1$ and $F = 1$.

- **Triangulation (Maximal Planar):** $E = 3V - 6$ exactly. Every face is a triangle.

- **Common Exam Trap:** A graph satisfying $E \leq 3V-6$ is **not necessarily planar**. Always double-check via subdivisions.

- **Kuratowski's Two Graphs:** Memorize — $K_5$ (5 vertices, 10 edges) and $K_{3,3}$ (6 vertices, 9 edges, bipartite).

- **Homeomorphism:** Two graphs are homeomorphic if obtained from a common graph by subdividing edges with degree-2 vertices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kuratowski's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kuratowski's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kuratowski's Theorem (1930)** states that *a finite graph is planar if and only if it does not contain a subgraph that is a subdivision of either the complete graph $K_5$ or the complete bipartite graph $K_{3,3}$.*

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme formal language:

> A graph $G = (V, E)$ is **non-planar** $\iff$ $G$ contains a subgraph $H$ that is homeomorphic to $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ (the **Kuratowski subgraphs**).

The two forbidden structures are universally called the **Kuratowski Forbidden Minors**:
- $K_5$ — the complete graph on **5 vertices** (10 edges).
- $K_{3,3}$ — the utility graph / complete bipartite graph on **3+3 vertices** (9 edges).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Forbidden Blueprints"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (the City Planner's Rule Book):**
> Imagine you are a city planner drawing roads on a flat map. You can keep adding intersections (vertices) and roads (edges) as long as nothing crosses. The theorem tells you there are only **two "poison" road patterns** that *force* a crossing no matter how cleverly you redraw them:
> 1. **$K_5$** — Five towns where *every* town is directly connected to *every other* town. With 10 mutual roads, you simply cannot lay them flat.
> 2. **$K_{3,3}$** — The "Utility Graph" — three houses and three utilities (water, gas, electricity) where each house needs a separate line to each utility. Nine unavoidable crossings.
>
> If your road network contains a *stretched-out, meandering copy* (a **subdivision**) of either of these two patterns, the map **cannot** be drawn without at least one overpass/underpass.

## 1.3 Why the Theorem Matters

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Decidability** | Reduces planarity testing to a finite search for $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ subdivisions. |
| **Minimality** | Both $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are *minimal* non-planar graphs — removing any edge makes them planar. |
| **Engineering Utility** | Used in **VLSI circuit design**, **PCB layout**, **road/railway planning**, and **network topology optimization**. |

## 1.4 Preliminaries Required

> [!NOTE]
> - **Planar Graph**: A graph that can be drawn in the plane with **no edge crossings** (other than at shared endpoints).
> - **Subdivision (Homeomorphism)**: Obtained by replacing edges with paths (inserting degree-2 vertices on existing edges).
> - **Subdivision of $G$**: A graph $H$ obtained from $G$ by repeatedly replacing an edge $uv$ with a path $u - x_1 - x_2 - \dots - x_k - v$, where each $x_i$ is a new vertex of degree 2.
> - Two graphs are **homeomorphic** if both are subdivisions of a common graph.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Flow of the Theorem

The theorem is a **biconditional** ($A \iff B$) with two directions:

### Direction 1 — Necessity ($\Rightarrow$)
- **Claim:** If $G$ is non-planar, then $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.
- **Intuition:** Any "first violation" of planarity must look like one of the two forbidden blueprints.
- **Why this works:** Both $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are *minimal* obstructions. They are the **smallest** non-planar graphs (in terms of edges).

### Direction 2 — Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$)
- **Claim:** If $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$, then $G$ is non-planar.
- **Intuition:** A subdivision does not change the "crossing essence" of a graph — degree-2 vertices are flexible pivots and cannot eliminate a fundamental crossing requirement.
- **Why this works:** If you could draw the subdivision in the plane, you could *contract* each subdivided path back to a single edge and obtain a crossing-free drawing of $K_5$ (or $K_{3,3}$) — which is impossible by **Euler's formula corollary** (see §2.3).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Forbidden Graph

### Why $K_5$ is Non-Planar
Apply **Euler's Formula** for connected planar graphs: $v - e + f = 2$.

$$v = 5, \quad e = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = e - v + 2 = 10 - 5 + 2 = 7$$

For a simple planar graph, each face has at least 3 edges on its boundary, and each edge borders 2 faces:

$$2e \geq 3f \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2(10) \geq 3(7) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20 \geq 21 \quad \text{— CONTRADICTION!}$$

Hence $K_5$ is non-planar. ∎

### Why $K_{3,3}$ is Non-Planar
For $K_{3,3}$: $v = 6$, $e = 9$, so $f = 9 - 6 + 2 = 5$.

$K_{3,3}$ is **bipartite** $\Rightarrow$ it has **no odd cycles** $\Rightarrow$ the shortest cycle has length **4** $\Rightarrow$ each face is bounded by $\geq 4$ edges.

$$2e \geq 4f \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2(9) \geq 4(5) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 18 \geq 20 \quad \text{— CONTRADICTION!}$$

Hence $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar. ∎

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Use in Kuratowski's Theorem |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $v$ | Number of vertices | $v = \vert V(G) \vert$ |
| $e$ | Number of edges | $e = \vert E(G) \vert$ |
| $f$ | Number of faces (planar) | $f = e - v + 2$ (Euler) |
| $K_n$ | Complete graph on $n$ vertices | $K_5$ is forbidden — non-planar |
| $K_{m,n}$ | Complete bipartite graph | $K_{3,3}$ is forbidden — non-planar |
| $2e \geq 3f$ | Face–edge inequality | Proves $K_5$ non-planarity |
| $2e \geq 4f$ | Bipartite face inequality | Proves $K_{3,3}$ non-planarity |
| $e \leq 3v - 6$ | Planar graph edge bound | $K_5$: $10 \not\leq 9$ — fails |
| $e \leq 2v - 4$ | Bipartite planar bound | $K_{3,3}$: $9 \not\leq 8$ — fails |
| $\delta(G)$ | Minimum degree | $\delta(K_5) = 4$, $\delta(K_{3,3}) = 3$ |
| Homeomorphic | Same via degree-2 subdivisions | Subdivisions preserve planarity status |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI / PCB Routing**: A chip layout is a planar graph; detecting $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ subdivisions tells the EDA tool *which nets must be routed on different layers*.
- **Railway & Highway Engineering**: The **Three-Utilities Problem** ($K_{3,3}$) is the classical reason why a fully connected service map requires a bridge or tunnel.
- **Network Topology**: SDN architects use Kuratowski's theorem to identify *bottleneck* sub-structures that force non-planar (multi-layer) deployments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying that a Given Graph is Non-Planar via Kuratowski

**Problem:** Show that the **Petersen Graph** $P$ contains a subdivision of $K_{3,3}$, and is therefore non-planar.

**Setup:** The Petersen graph has $v = 10$, $e = 15$.

### Step 1 — Check the edge-count bound
For a simple planar graph: $e \leq 3v - 6$.

$$3v - 6 = 3(10) - 6 = 24$$

$$e = 15 \leq 24$$

The edge bound is **not violated**, so we cannot conclude non-planarity from this alone. We must find a Kuratowski subgraph explicitly.

### Step 2 — Label the Petersen Graph
The standard Petersen graph has an *outer 5-cycle* $\{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5\}$ and an *inner pentagram* $\{u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4, u_5\}$, with edges $v_i u_i$ connecting them.

### Step 3 — Identify a $K_{3,3}$ Subdivision
Pick the bipartite partition:
- **Set $A$** = $\{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$
- **Set $B$** = $\{u_1, u_3, u_5\}$

Now trace the 9 required "cross" connections. Many exist as multi-vertex paths:

| Required Edge of $K_{3,3}$ | Path in Petersen Graph |
|:---:|:---|
| $v_1 \to u_1$ | $v_1 - u_1$ (direct) |
| $v_1 \to u_3$ | $v_1 - v_2 - v_3 - u_3$ |
| $v_1 \to u_5$ | $v_1 - v_5 - u_5$ |
| $v_2 \to u_1$ | $v_2 - v_1 - u_1$ |
| $v_2 \to u_3$ | $v_2 - v_3 - u_3$ (direct) |
| $v_2 \to u_5$ | $v_2 - v_1 - v_5 - u_5$ |
| $v_3 \to u_1$ | $v_3 - v_4 - v_5 - v_1 - u_1$ |
| $v_3 \to u_3$ | $v_3 - u_3$ (direct) |
| $v_3 \to u_5$ | $v_3 - v_4 - v_5 - u_5$ |

All 9 paths are internally vertex-disjoint and edge-disjoint. This is a **subdivision of $K_{3,3}$**.

### Step 4 — Apply the Theorem
By Kuratowski's theorem, the Petersen graph is **non-planar**. ∎

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Constructing a $K_5$ Subdivision

**Problem:** Prove that the graph $G$ shown below (a "diamond" with three extra vertices on pendant edges) is non-planar.

Vertices: $\{a, b, c, d, e\}$, with edges $ab, ac, ad, ae, bc, bd, be, cd, ce, de$ **— but** with one edge, say $ad$, replaced by the path $a - x - d$ (inserted degree-2 vertex $x$).

### Step 1 — Identify the $K_5$ Subdivision
Contract the path $a - x - d$ back to a single edge $ad$. We obtain all $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ edges of $K_5$.

Hence $G$ is a **subdivision of $K_5$** (with $x$ as the only degree-2 insertion).

### Step 2 — Conclusion
By Kuratowski's theorem (sufficiency direction), $G$ is **non-planar**. ∎

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving the Sufficiency Direction (Sketch)

**Claim:** If $H$ is a subdivision of a non-planar graph $G$, then $H$ is non-planar.

### Step 1 — Assume for contradiction
Suppose $H$ is planar. Then there exists a planar embedding $\phi : H \to \mathbb{R}^2$ with no edge crossings.

### Step 2 — Contract all subdivided paths
For each path $u - x_1 - x_2 - \dots - x_k - v$ in $H$ that corresponds to a single edge $uv$ in $G$, contract each $x_i$ (a degree-2 vertex) onto $u$ (or $v$).

Formally, define the equivalence relation: $x_i \sim u$ for all subdivision vertices $x_i$ on that path.

### Step 3 — Verify the contraction preserves planarity
Subdividing and contracting are **inverse operations** on the embedding:
- A degree-2 vertex $x_i$ on a curve can be slid along the curve to a neighbor without creating a crossing.
- Repeatedly sliding all subdivision vertices onto endpoints yields a planar embedding of $G$.

### Step 4 — Reach Contradiction
We have constructed a planar embedding of $G$. But $G$ is non-planar by hypothesis (e.g., $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$).

$$\therefore \;\; H \text{ must be non-planar.} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Is my graph planar?"

| Test Performed | Result | Conclusion |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| $e > 3v - 6$ | TRUE | Non-planar (instant) |
| $e > 2v - 4$ AND graph is bipartite | TRUE | Non-planar (instant) |
| Contains $K_5$ as subgraph | TRUE | Non-planar (Kuratowski) |
| Contains $K_{3,3}$ as subgraph | TRUE | Non-planar (Kuratowski) |
| Contains subdivision of $K_5$ | TRUE | Non-planar (Kuratowski) |
| Contains subdivision of $K_{3,3}$ | TRUE | Non-planar (Kuratowski) |
| None of the above found | — | **Inconclusive** — use Hopcroft–Tarjan or similar $O(v)$ planarity test |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Two Forbidden Minors — Schematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph K5["K5 Complete Graph"]
        A1["v1"] --- A2["v2"]
        A1 --- A3["v3"]
        A1 --- A4["v4"]
        A1 --- A5["v5"]
        A2 --- A3
        A2 --- A4
        A2 --- A5
        A3 --- A4
        A3 --- A5
        A4 --- A5
    end
    subgraph K33["K33 Bipartite Utility Graph"]
        B1["House H1"] --- C1["Utility U1"]
        B1 --- C2["Utility U2"]
        B1 --- C3["Utility U3"]
        B2["House H2"] --- C1
        B2 --- C2
        B2 --- C3
        B3["House H3"] --- C1
        B3 --- C2
        B3 --- C3
    end
    K5 --> R["Kuratowski Theorem: FORBIDDEN MINORS"]
    K33 --> R
    R --> NP["Any graph containing a subdivision of K5 or K33 is NON-PLANAR"]
```

## 4.2 Subdivision Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Original Graph G with edge uv"] --> Insert["Insert degree-2 vertex x on edge uv"]
    Insert["New graph H: path u-x-v"] --> Planarity{"Can H be drawn\nwithout crossings?"}
    Planarity -- "NO" --> Conclusion["H is non-planar\nH is a subdivision of G\nG is non-planar (by Kuratowski)"]
    Planarity -- "YES" --> Planar["H is planar\nG (after contracting x) is also planar\n→ G is planar"]
```

## 4.3 The Petersen Graph — $K_{3,3}$ Subdivision Detection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Petersen Graph v=10, e=15"] --> Check1{"e > 3v - 6 ?\n15 > 24 ?"}
    Check1 -- "NO" --> Search["Search for K5 or K33 subdivision"]
    Search --> Found["Found K33 subdivision\n(via paths through outer cycle)"]
    Found --> Result["NON-PLANAR\n(by Kuratowski's Theorem)"]
    Check1 -- "YES (would be)" --> Immediate["Immediate non-planarity"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Planarity Testing (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G["Input Graph G"] --> Q1{"Has loop or\nmulti-edge?"}
    Q1 -- "YES multi-edge" --> S1["Possibly non-planar\nApply Kuratowski"]
    Q1 -- "NO" --> Q2{"e > 3v - 6 ?"}
    Q2 -- "YES" --> NP1["NON-PLANAR"]
    Q2 -- "NO" --> Q3{"G is bipartite\nAND e > 2v - 4 ?"}
    Q3 -- "YES" --> NP2["NON-PLANAR"]
    Q3 -- "NO" --> Q4{"Contains subdivision\nof K5 or K33 ?"}
    Q4 -- "YES" --> NP3["NON-PLANAR\n(Kuratowski)"]
    Q4 -- "NO" --> Q5{"Connected components\n> 1 ?"}
    Q5 -- "YES" --> Recurse["Test each component\nseparately"]
    Q5 -- "NO" --> PlanarCheck["Use Hopcroft-Tarjan\nO(v) planarity test"]

    PlanarCheck --> NP["NON-PLANAR"]:::red
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Kuratowski's Theorem. Why is it called a *characterization* theorem?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Kuratowski's Theorem:** *A graph $G$ is planar if and only if $G$ does not contain a subgraph that is a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.*
>
> It is called a **characterization theorem** because it provides a *necessary and sufficient condition* — i.e., a complete and precise rule — that *exactly* identifies the class of planar graphs in terms of forbidden substructures. The two forbidden subgraphs $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are called the **Kuratowski forbidden minors**.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for subdivision clarification, 1 mark for "characterization" justification.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "subdivision of a graph" with an example. Are $G$ and any subdivision of $G$ always of the same planarity status?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **subdivision** of a graph $G$ is obtained by replacing one or more edges of $G$ with paths, inserting new vertices of degree 2. Formally, edge $uv$ is replaced by $u - x_1 - x_2 - \dots - x_k - v$.
>
> **Example:** $K_3$ (a triangle) subdivided by adding a vertex $x$ on edge $ab$ yields the path $a - x - b$ together with the other two edges $ac, bc$.
>
> **Yes** — $G$ and any subdivision of $G$ have the **same planarity status**. This is precisely the content of the *sufficiency* direction of Kuratowski's theorem: if $G$ is non-planar, every subdivision is non-planar, and vice versa.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition + example, 1.5 marks for planarity invariance with reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `$K_5$ Subdivision Focus`

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question Paper]**
> **(a)** Define a planar graph. Prove using Euler's formula that $K_5$ is non-planar. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the concept of a *subdivision* of a graph. Show, with a labelled diagram, how a particular 7-vertex graph $G$ contains a subdivision of $K_5$, and hence conclude that $G$ is non-planar by Kuratowski's theorem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Planar Graph Definition and Proof that $K_5$ is Non-Planar**

A graph $G$ is **planar** if it can be drawn in the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that no two edges cross except at their common endpoints.

**Proof that $K_5$ is non-planar (by contradiction using Euler's formula):**

For $K_5$: $v = 5$ vertices, $e = \binom{5}{2} = 10$ edges.

Assume, for contradiction, that $K_5$ is planar. By **Euler's formula** for connected planar graphs:

$$v - e + f = 2$$

Substituting:

$$5 - 10 + f = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = 7$$

Since $K_5$ is a **simple graph**, every face is bounded by at least 3 edges, and each edge is on the boundary of exactly 2 faces. Counting edge–face incidences:

$$2e \geq 3f \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2(10) \geq 3(7) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20 \geq 21$$

This is a **contradiction**. Hence $K_5$ is non-planar. ∎

*(7 marks — 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for Euler derivation, 2 marks for contradiction.)*

**(b) Subdivision and $K_5$ Subdivision Example**

**Definition of Subdivision:**
A **subdivision** of a graph $G$ is a graph $H$ obtained by replacing some edges $uv$ of $G$ with paths $u - x_1 - x_2 - \dots - x_k - v$, where each $x_i$ is a new vertex of degree 2.

**Example Construction:**

Let $G$ be the graph with vertex set $V = \{a, b, c, d, e, p, q\}$ and edge set:
- Triangle core: $ab, ac, bc$
- Pendant subdivisions: $ad' \to a-p-d$, $ae' \to a-q-e$, $bd', cd', be', ce'$ adjusted accordingly.

Take a simpler construction: Start with $K_5$ on $\{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and subdivide **one single edge**, say $de$, by inserting a new vertex $f$ of degree 2, replacing $de$ with the path $d - f - e$.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
        a
       /|\
      / | \
     b--c  d
      \ |  |
       \|  f
        e--+
```

The resulting graph $G'$ has:
- $v = 6$, $e = 10$
- Vertices $a, b, c, d, e$ with all mutual edges **except** $de$, which is now the path $d - f - e$.

**Identification of the $K_5$ Subdivision:**

Contract the degree-2 vertex $f$ back onto the edge $de$. We recover the original $K_5$ on $\{a, b, c, d, e\}$. Hence $G'$ is a **subdivision of $K_5$**.

**Conclusion by Kuratowski's Theorem:**

By the *sufficiency* direction of Kuratowski's theorem, any graph containing a subdivision of $K_5$ is non-planar. Therefore, $G'$ is non-planar. ∎

*(7 marks — 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for example + diagram, 2 marks for Kuratowski application.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `$K_{3,3}$ Subdivision Focus`

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Question Paper]**
> **(a)** What is the Utility Graph $K_{3,3}$? Prove using Euler's formula that $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** State and explain Kuratowski's Theorem. Given a graph $G$ with $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6, v_7\}$ and edge set as specified in the figure, identify a subdivision of $K_{3,3}$ in $G$ and prove non-planarity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Utility Graph and its Non-Planarity**

The **Utility Graph** $K_{3,3}$ is the complete bipartite graph with bipartition $\{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ and $\{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$: each $x_i$ is connected to each $y_j$ by a unique edge. It has $v = 6$ vertices, $e = 3 \times 3 = 9$ edges, and represents the classical *Three Utilities Problem* (three houses connected to three utilities).

**Proof of Non-Planarity (by contradiction):**

Assume $K_{3,3}$ is planar. Since $K_{3,3}$ is **connected**, by Euler's formula:

$$v - e + f = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6 - 9 + f = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = 5$$

Since $K_{3,3}$ is **bipartite**, it contains **no odd cycles**. Therefore the smallest cycle has length 4, meaning every face is bounded by at least 4 edges. Counting edge–face incidences:

$$2e \geq 4f \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2(9) \geq 4(5) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 18 \geq 20$$

This is a **contradiction**. Hence $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar. ∎

*(7 marks — 2 marks for utility graph description, 3 marks for Euler + bipartite inequality, 2 marks for contradiction.)*

**(b) Kuratowski's Theorem — Statement and Application**

**Statement of Kuratowski's Theorem:**

> A finite graph $G$ is planar if and only if $G$ does **not** contain a subgraph that is a **subdivision** of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.

**Explanation:**
- **"If"** (sufficiency): Any graph containing a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ is automatically non-planar (since subdivisions preserve planarity status).
- **"Only if"** (necessity): Every non-planar graph *must* contain such a subdivision — these are the **only** obstructions to planarity.

**Identification of a $K_{3,3}$ Subdivision in $G$:**

Consider the graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6, v_7\}$ and the following edge structure (standard KTU textbook example):
- Outer cycle: $v_1 v_2, v_2 v_3, v_3 v_4, v_4 v_5, v_5 v_1$
- Diagonals: $v_1 v_3, v_1 v_4, v_2 v_4, v_2 v_5, v_3 v_5$
- Central hub: $v_6$ connected to $v_2, v_3, v_4$; $v_7$ connected to $v_1, v_5, v_6$.

Set the bipartition:
- $A = \{v_1, v_6, v_3\}$
- $B = \{v_2, v_5, v_4\}$

Verify 9 disjoint paths between $A$ and $B$:

| From $\to$ To | Path in $G$ |
|:---:|:---|
| $v_1 \to v_2$ | $v_1 - v_2$ (direct) |
| $v_1 \to v_5$ | $v_1 - v_5$ (direct) |
| $v_1 \to v_4$ | $v_1 - v_4$ (direct) |
| $v_6 \to v_2$ | $v_6 - v_2$ (direct) |
| $v_6 \to v_5$ | $v_6 - v_7 - v_5$ |
| $v_6 \to v_4$ | $v_6 - v_4$ (direct) |
| $v_3 \to v_2$ | $v_3 - v_2$ (direct) |
| $v_3 \to v_5$ | $v_3 - v_5$ (direct) |
| $v_3 \to v_4$ | $v_3 - v_4$ (direct) |

All 9 paths are internally vertex-disjoint. Therefore, $G$ contains a **subdivision of $K_{3,3}$**.

**Conclusion:** By Kuratowski's theorem, $G$ is non-planar. ∎

*(7 marks — 2 marks for statement, 2 marks for bipartition choice, 3 marks for path enumeration and conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "subgraph" with "subdivision"** — A graph may *not* contain $K_5$ as a subgraph but *does* contain a subdivision of $K_5$ (e.g., with extra degree-2 vertices). Examiners explicitly test this distinction. **(Lose up to 3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "homeomorphic" condition** — Writing "$G$ contains $K_5$" instead of "$G$ contains a *subdivision* of $K_5$" is incomplete and will lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Not stating Euler's formula prerequisite** — When proving $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar, always *first* state Euler's formula, then *assume for contradiction*, then *derive the face inequality* ($2e \geq 3f$ or $2e \geq 4f$). Skipping the contradiction setup loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Bipartite face inequality misuse** — Using $2e \geq 3f$ for $K_{3,3}$ is **wrong**; bipartite graphs require $2e \geq 4f$ because they have no triangles.
>
> 5. **Not proving "internal vertex-disjointness"** — When showing a subdivision of $K_{3,3}$, the 9 paths must be **mutually internally vertex-disjoint**. Drawing overlapping paths is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Kuratowski's Theorem**
>
> - **Statement:** $G$ is planar $\iff$ $G$ does **not** contain a subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$.
> - **Two forbidden minors:** $K_5$ (5 vertices, 10 edges) and $K_{3,3}$ (utility graph, 9 edges).
> - **$K_5$ is non-planar** because $2e = 20 < 21 = 3f$ (Euler gives $f=7$, simple graph needs $2e \geq 3f$).
> - **$K_{3,3}$ is non-planar** because it is bipartite (no triangles) so $2e \geq 4f$, but $18 < 20$.
> - **Subdivision** = inserting degree-2 vertices on edges. Subdivisions **preserve** planarity status.
> - **Sufficiency direction** proof idea: a planar embedding of a subdivision can be "contracted" (sliding degree-2 vertices) to give a planar embedding of the original.
> - **Necessity direction** (deep): any minimal non-planar graph must be $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ up to homeomorphism (proved via Wagner's theorem or induction).
> - **Edge bounds to memorize:**
>   - Planar: $e \leq 3v - 6$
>   - Bipartite planar: $e \leq 2v - 4$
> - **Algorithm connection:** Kuratowski's theorem is the *theoretical foundation* for $O(v)$-time planarity testing (Hopcroft–Tarjan, Boyer–Myrvold).
> - **Engineering applications:** VLSI layout, PCB routing, network design, transportation planning.
> - **Examiner tip:** Always state the biconditional ($\iff$) — most students only write the "if" direction.
> - **Pitfall:** "Subdivision of $K_5$" is **weaker** than "$K_5$ as subgraph" — many non-planar graphs have no $K_5$ subgraph but have a $K_5$ subdivision.
> - **Historical credit:** Kazimierz Kuratowski (Polish mathematician, 1930) — published in *Fundamenta Mathematicae*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wagner's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wagner's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Wagner's Theorem — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Statement of Wagner's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wagner's Theorem (K. Wagner, 1937):**
> A finite graph $G$ is **planar** if and only if $G$ contains neither $K_5$ (the complete graph on 5 vertices) nor $K_{3,3}$ (the complete bipartite graph on 3+3 vertices) as a **minor**.

In set-theoretic notation:

$$G \text{ is planar} \iff K_5 \not\preceq_m G \;\;\text{and}\;\; K_{3,3} \not\preceq_m G$$

where the symbol $\preceq_m$ denotes the **minor relation** (i.e., "is a minor of").

This is the **minor-form** characterization of planar graphs and is a close cousin of **Kuratowski's Theorem** (which uses *subdivisions / topological minors* instead of *minors*).

---

### 1.2 Preliminary Concepts (Syllabus Prerequisite)

> [!NOTE]
> Before applying Wagner's Theorem, the student must be fluent in the following prerequisite definitions.

**Definition 1 — Edge Contraction:**
Given a graph $G$ and an edge $e = uv$, the operation of **edge contraction** merges the two endpoints $u$ and $v$ into a single new vertex, deletes $e$, and replaces every other edge incident to $u$ or $v$ with an edge incident to the new merged vertex. Self-loops and multi-edges are removed. The result is denoted $G / e$.

**Definition 2 — Graph Minor:**
A graph $H$ is a **minor** of $G$, written $H \preceq_m G$, if $H$ can be obtained from $G$ by a finite sequence of:
- **Vertex deletion** (removing a vertex and its incident edges),
- **Edge deletion** (removing an edge), and
- **Edge contraction** (collapsing an edge).

Equivalently, $H \preceq_m G$ iff there exist pairwise vertex-disjoint connected subgraphs $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_{h}$ of $G$ such that for every edge $xy$ of $H$, at least one edge of $G$ joins a vertex in $G_x$ to a vertex in $G_y$. The subgraphs $G_i$ are called the **branch sets**.

**Definition 3 — Planar Graph:**
A graph $G$ is **planar** if it can be embedded in the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that no two edges cross (except at common endpoints).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine you are packing a complex wiring diagram onto a flat circuit board. You are allowed to:
- **Throw away wires** you don't need (edge deletion),
- **Remove unused components** (vertex deletion), and
- **Solder two adjacent components together**, merging their pins (edge contraction).

> [!TIP]
> **The key insight:** If after these "cheap" operations you can still reduce the circuit down to one of just **two impossible shapes** — namely the 5-pointed star $K_5$ or the "utility-network" $K_{3,3}$ — then the original circuit is **fundamentally non-planar**, no matter how cleverly you rerouted the wires.

Conversely, if no such reduction is possible, you are *guaranteed* a flat layout exists. Wagner's Theorem is essentially a precise mathematical statement of this intuition.

---

### 1.4 Why KTU 2024 Includes This Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wagner's Theorem is foundational for the **Robertson–Seymour Graph Minor Theorem** (the proof of Wagner's Conjecture, 2004), which states that in any infinite family of finite graphs closed under taking minors, only **finitely many** minimal forbidden minors exist. This is a flagship result connecting structural graph theory to topological graph theory and is examinable under Module 4 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Architecture of the Theorem

Wagner's Theorem is a biconditional statement. We must prove both directions:

| Direction | Statement | Proof Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(⇐) Sufficiency / "Only if"** | If $G$ has $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a minor, then $G$ is non-planar. | **Minors preserve non-planarity.** Contracting and deleting edges/vertices cannot turn a non-planar graph into a planar one. |
| **(⇒) Necessity / "If"** | If $G$ is non-planar, then $G$ contains $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a minor. | **Induction on $\|V(G)\|$** combined with a degree-based case split (small-degree vertex contraction) and a discharging / Euler-formula argument (high-degree regime). |

---

## 2.2 Why the Two Forbidden Minors?

Both $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are themselves **non-planar**, as can be verified from Euler's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_5 &: \quad V = 5, \;\; E = \binom{5}{2} = 10 \\
     &:\quad 3V - 6 = 3(5) - 6 = 9 < 10 = E \quad \Rightarrow \text{non-planar} \\[4pt]
K_{3,3} &: \quad V = 6, \;\; E = (3)(3) = 9 \\
         &:\quad \text{Bipartite, so } 2V - 4 = 2(6) - 4 = 8 < 9 = E \quad \Rightarrow \text{non-planar}
\end{aligned}
$$

The structural reason both appear is that:
- $K_5$ is the **smallest vertex-transitive non-planar graph**.
- $K_{3,3}$ is the **smallest non-planar bipartite graph** (and is the famous "three-utilities puzzle").

---

## 2.3 Properties of the Minor Relation Used in the Proof

> [!NOTE]
> The proof relies critically on three closure properties of the minor relation.

1. **Reflexivity:** $G \preceq_m G$ (the trivial sequence of operations does nothing).
2. **Transitivity:** If $H \preceq_m G$ and $G \preceq_m F$, then $H \preceq_m F$.
3. **Planarity is closed under minors:** If $G$ is planar, then every minor of $G$ is planar. (Contraction / deletion obviously cannot introduce crossings.)

From (3) we obtain the contrapositive: **if $H$ is non-planar and $H \preceq_m G$, then $G$ is non-planar.** This proves the (⇐) direction immediately.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement | Domain of Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Euler's Formula** | $V - E + F = 2$ | Connected planar graphs |
| 2 | **Planar edge bound (general)** | $E \le 3V - 6$ | Simple connected planar, $V \ge 3$ |
| 3 | **Planar edge bound (triangle-free)** | $E \le 2V - 4$ | Simple connected bipartite planar, $V \ge 3$ |
| 4 | **Handshake Lemma** | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2E$ | All graphs |
| 5 | **Face-degree identity** | $\sum_{f \in F} \deg(f) = 2E$ | Planar graphs |
| 6 | **Kuratowski's Theorem** | $G$ planar $\iff$ no subdivision of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ | All graphs |
| 7 | **Wagner's Theorem** | $G$ planar $\iff$ $K_5, K_{3,3} \not\preceq_m G$ | All finite graphs |
| 8 | **Contraction of $e$** | $V(G/e) = V - 1, \;\; E(G/e) = E - 1$ | Single edge contraction |
| 9 | **K₅ parameters** | $V = 5, \;\; E = 10$ | $K_5$ |
| 10 | **K₃,₃ parameters** | $V = 6, \;\; E = 9$ | $K_{3,3}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Wagner's / Kuratowski's forbidden-minor characterizations are deployed in **VLSI circuit design** (deciding if a netlist is single-layer routable), **printed circuit board (PCB) layout**, and **automated network-topology verification** in industrial graph-tooling software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The (⇐) Direction — "Non-planarity persists through minors"

We must show: if $K_5 \preceq_m G$ (or $K_{3,3} \preceq_m G$), then $G$ is non-planar.

**Proof.**

> Step 1. By definition of the minor relation, $K_5$ can be obtained from $G$ by some sequence of edge deletions, vertex deletions, and edge contractions. Call this derived graph $H = K_5$ (in the first case).

> Step 2. We invoke the closure property: *if $G'$ is a minor of $G$, then $G$ planar $\Rightarrow$ $G'$ planar.* Equivalently (contrapositive), $G'$ non-planar $\Rightarrow$ $G$ non-planar.

> Step 3. Since $K_5$ is non-planar (verified by Euler: $10 > 9 = 3(5) - 6$), the contrapositive in Step 2 forces $G$ to be non-planar.

> Step 4. The identical argument works with $K_{3,3}$ in place of $K_5$ (using the bipartite bound $9 > 8 = 2(6) - 4$).

Hence $G$ non-planar. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 The (⇒) Direction — "Every non-planar graph has a $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor"

This is the non-trivial direction. The classical proof (Wagner, 1937) proceeds by **induction on the number of vertices $n$** of a *minimal counterexample* $G$.

### Setup: Base Case

> **Base case $n = 5$:** A non-planar graph on 5 vertices with no $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor. Inspection of all non-planar graphs on $\le 5$ vertices shows the only such graph is $K_5$ itself. So no counterexample exists. $\checkmark$

> **Base case $n = 6$:** Any non-planar graph on 6 vertices without a $K_{3,3}$ minor must be $K_5$ plus an attached vertex, which is handled by induction via edge contraction (see Step 1 of the inductive step). $\checkmark$

### Inductive Step (Case Split)

Assume the theorem holds for all non-planar graphs with fewer than $n$ vertices; let $G$ be a non-planar graph on $n$ vertices that contains **no $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor**. We derive a contradiction.

#### Case A — $G$ has a vertex of degree $\le 3$

> Step A.1. Let $v \in V(G)$ with $\deg(v) \le 3$.

> Step A.2. Form $G' = G - v$ (delete $v$ and its incident edges). Then $G'$ has $n - 1$ vertices.

> Step A.3. **Claim:** $G'$ is still non-planar. Suppose for contradiction that $G'$ is planar with embedding $\pi'$. We attempt to re-insert $v$. Since $v$ has $\le 3$ neighbors, by the **planar re-insertion lemma** we can place $v$ inside a face incident to all three neighbors (at most 3). Such a face exists in any planar embedding of $G'$ that keeps the neighbors on a common face boundary. This contradicts the non-planarity of $G$.

> Step A.4. By induction, $G'$ contains a $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor. Since $G' \preceq_m G$ (vertex deletion is one of the allowed operations), this minor is also a minor of $G$, contradiction. $\blacksquare$

#### Case B — Every vertex of $G$ has degree $\ge 4$

> Step B.1. By the Handshake Lemma, $2E = \sum_v \deg(v) \ge 4n$, so $E \ge 2n$.

> Step B.2. Suppose, for contradiction, that $G$ is **planar**. Then by Euler's formula,
> $$V - E + F = 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F = 2 - n + E \ge 2 - n + 2n = n + 2.$$

> Step B.3. By the face-degree identity, $\sum_{f \in F} \deg(f) = 2E \ge 4n$. Hence the **average face degree** is
> $$\bar{\deg}(f) = \frac{2E}{F} \le \frac{2E}{n+2}.$$

> Step B.4. We use a **discharging argument**: assign each face $f$ a charge of $\deg(f) - 4$, and each vertex $v$ a charge of $\deg(v) - 4$. The total charge is
> $$\sum_f (\deg(f) - 4) + \sum_v (\deg(v) - 4) = 2E - 4F + 2E - 4V = 4E - 4(V + F) = 4E - 4(E+2) = -8.$$

> Step B.5. **Discharging rule:** transfer $\tfrac{1}{2}$ unit of charge from each face to each incident vertex. Each face $f$ ends with charge $\deg(f) - 4 - \tfrac{\deg(f)}{2} = \tfrac{\deg(f)}{2} - 4$. Each vertex $v$ ends with charge $\deg(v) - 4 + \tfrac{\deg(v)}{2} = \tfrac{3\deg(v)}{2} - 4$.

> Step B.6. A non-negative final face charge requires $\deg(f) \ge 8$ for every face. A non-negative final vertex charge requires $\deg(v) \ge \tfrac{8}{3}$, i.e., $\deg(v) \ge 3$ — which is true by Case B assumption.

> Step B.7. Therefore the final vertex charges are all $\ge 0$ in our case. For the total charge to be $-8$, **at least one face must have negative final charge**, i.e., $\deg(f) < 8$, i.e., a face of length 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 must exist.

> Step B.8. **Sub-case B.i — A triangular face $\{a, b, c\}$ exists.**
> Contract the edge $bc$ to get $G / bc$. The new vertex $b' = bc$ has degree $\ge 3$ (it absorbed at least one neighbour of $b$ and one of $c$, plus the edge $ab$). The contracted graph $G / bc$ has $n - 1$ vertices and is still non-planar (contraction cannot eliminate crossings). By induction, $G / bc$ contains a $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor, hence so does $G$. $\checkmark$

> Step B.9. **Sub-case B.ii — A face of length 4, 5, 6, or 7 exists with no triangle.**
> Use an *augmenting path* argument in the planar dual to find an edge whose contraction reduces the face count while preserving non-planarity and the minimum-degree $\ge 4$ property. The detailed sub-step is omitted in KTU board answers (cite the Lemma on small-face contraction).

> Step B.10. All sub-cases yield a $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor, contradicting the assumption. Hence Case B is impossible. $\blacksquare$

Combining Case A and Case B, no non-planar graph on $n$ vertices can avoid both minors, completing the induction.

### Final Statement of the (⇒) Proof

$$
\boxed{
G \text{ is non-planar } \;\Longrightarrow\; K_5 \preceq_m G \;\;\text{or}\;\; K_{3,3} \preceq_m G.
}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Detecting a $K_{3,3}$ Minor

> [!TIP]
> **Problem.** Show that the Petersen graph $P$ contains $K_{3,3}$ as a minor and is therefore non-planar.

> **Solution.**
> Label the outer 5-cycle as $u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4, u_5$ and the inner 5-cycle (pentagram) as $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5$, where each $v_i$ is joined to $u_i$ and $u_{i+2}$ (indices mod 5). Form the following branch sets:
> $$B_1 = \{u_1, v_1\}, \quad B_2 = \{u_2, v_2\}, \quad B_3 = \{u_3, v_3\}, \quad B_4 = \{u_4, v_4\}, \quad B_5 = \{u_5, v_5\}.$$
> (Each $B_i$ induces a path of length 1 in $P$, hence is connected.) Collapse each $B_i$ to a single vertex $w_i$. One can verify that the resulting minor is $K_5$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Graph — The Two Forbidden Minors

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[K5 COMPLETE GRAPH]:::forbidden
    A2[K3,3 UTILITY GRAPH]:::forbidden
    A3[FINITE GRAPH G]:::input
    A4[CHECK MINOR RELATION]:::proc
    A5[PLANAR]:::planar
    A6[NON PLANAR]:::nonplanar
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A4 --> A6
    A1 -.NON PLANAR.-> A6
    A2 -.NON PLANAR.-> A6
    A4 -- G has K5 minor --> A6
    A4 -- G has K3,3 minor --> A6
    A4 -- G has neither --> A5
    classDef forbidden fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef planar fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef nonplanar fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a93226,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Edge Contraction Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[GRAPH G]:::input
    E1[Edge e = uv]:::edge
    C1[STEP 1: Identify endpoints u and v]:::step
    C2[STEP 2: Merge u and v into new vertex w]:::step
    C3[STEP 3: Replace every edge ux with wx and vx with wx]:::step
    C4[STEP 4: Remove self-loops and multi-edges]:::step
    G1[CONTRACTED GRAPH G / e]:::output
    G0 --> E1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> G1
    classDef input fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef edge fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#f39c12,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Proof Strategy of the (⇒) Direction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START: Non-planar G on n vertices]:::input
    S1{Base case n at most 5 or 6?}:::decision
    S2[Trivially K5 or K3,3 — no counterexample]:::base
    S3{Existence of vertex with degree at most 3?}:::decision
    S4[Case A: Delete v, apply induction, lift minor back]:::caseA
    S5[Case B: Every vertex has degree at least 4]:::caseB
    S6[Apply Handshake and Euler: E at least 2n, F at least n+2]:::formula
    S7[Discharging argument: total charge equals -8]:::discharge
    S8{Small face of length 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 exists?}:::decision
    S9[Contract an edge to reduce to induction hypothesis]:::contract
    S10[CONTRADICTION — hence K5 or K3,3 minor must exist]:::conclude
    S0 --> S1
    S1 -- yes --> S2 --> S10
    S1 -- no --> S3
    S3 -- yes --> S4 --> S10
    S3 -- no --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S8 -- yes --> S9 --> S10
    S8 -- no --> S7
    classDef input fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef caseA fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#d68910,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef caseB fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#cb4335,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef formula fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef discharge fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef contract fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conclude fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Minor Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Graph G]:::input
    P2[Branch set search: find 5 or 6 vertex-disjoint connected subgraphs]:::search
    P3[Validate connectivity of each branch set]:::check
    P4[Validate inter-branch adjacency matches K5 or K3,3 edge set]:::check
    P5{All checks pass?}:::decision
    P6[OUTPUT: K5 or K3,3 minor found — G is non-planar]:::positive
    P7[OUTPUT: No forbidden minor found — proceed to planarity test]:::negative
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P5 -- yes --> P6
    P5 -- no --> P7
    classDef input fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef search fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#f39c12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#d68910,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef negative fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
