<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** State Wagner's Theorem. How does it differ from Kuratowski's Theorem?

**Model Answer:**
Wagner's Theorem states that a finite graph $G$ is planar if and only if $G$ contains neither $K_5$ nor $K_{3,3}$ as a minor. It differs from Kuratowski's Theorem, which states that $G$ is planar if and only if $G$ contains no **subdivision** (topological minor) of $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$. The key distinction is that Kuratowski uses *edge subdivision*, while Wagner uses *edge contraction and vertex/edge deletion* as the allowed operations.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** Define the "minor" relation on graphs. List the three elementary operations used to obtain a minor.

**Model Answer:**
A graph $H$ is a **minor** of $G$, denoted $H \preceq_m G$, if $H$ can be obtained from $G$ by a sequence of three elementary operations:
1. **Vertex deletion** — removing a vertex and all its incident edges,
2. **Edge deletion** — removing an edge, and
3. **Edge contraction** — merging the two endpoints of an edge into a single vertex and removing the resulting loop / multi-edges.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Prove the **(⇐) direction** of Wagner's Theorem: *If a graph $G$ has $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a minor, then $G$ is non-planar.*
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Illustrate with a small example how a $K_{3,3}$ minor can be identified in a given graph using branch sets.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

We must show: $K_5 \preceq_m G$ or $K_{3,3} \preceq_m G \Rightarrow G$ is non-planar.

> **Step 1 (Contraction Lemma).** *If $G'$ is obtained from $G$ by a single edge contraction, then $G$ planar $\Rightarrow$ $G'$ planar.*
> *Proof sketch:* Given a planar embedding of $G$, contracting an edge $e = uv$ in that embedding produces an embedding of $G/e$ by collapsing the two endpoints into one. Crossings of edges in $G$ would already be crossings in $G$ — they cannot be introduced by contraction. Hence planarity is preserved.

> **Step 2 (Deletion Lemma).** *If $G'$ is obtained from $G$ by deleting a vertex or an edge, then $G$ planar $\Rightarrow$ $G'$ planar.*
> *Proof sketch:* Deleting an edge or vertex from a planar embedding leaves the remaining graph embedded without crossings.

> **Step 3 (Induction on operations).** Since $K_5$ (or $K_{3,3}$) is a minor of $G$, there is a sequence of allowed operations transforming $G$ into $K_5$ (or $K_{3,3}$). By Steps 1 and 2, the planarity of $G$ would imply the planarity of $K_5$ (resp. $K_{3,3}$) — a contradiction, since both are non-planar.

> **Step 4 (Verification of non-planarity of the two forbidden minors).**
> $$K_5: \quad V = 5, \;\; E = 10 > 3(5) - 6 = 9 \Rightarrow \text{non-planar}.$$
> $$K_{3,3}: \quad V = 6, \;\; E = 9 > 2(6) - 4 = 8 \Rightarrow \text{non-planar (bipartite bound)}.$$

> **Conclusion.** Since $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are both non-planar, and the minor operations cannot destroy planarity, $G$ must be non-planar. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Example graph:** Let $H$ be the graph formed by two disjoint triangles $\{a_1, b_1, c_1\}$ and $\{a_2, b_2, c_2\}$ with three additional cross-edges $a_1 a_2$, $b_1 b_2$, $c_1 c_2$.

**Branch sets for a $K_{3,3}$ minor:**
- $B_1 = \{a_1, a_2\}$,
- $B_2 = \{b_1, b_2\}$,
- $B_3 = \{c_1, c_2\}$.

Each $B_i$ is connected (via the cross-edge). For every $i \ne j$ there is an edge joining $B_i$ to $B_j$ (e.g., $a_1 b_1$ within the first triangle, and $a_2 b_2$ within the second). After collapsing each $B_i$ to a single vertex, the resulting 3-vertex multigraph has all 9 inter-set edges present, giving $K_{3,3}$. Hence $H$ is non-planar by Wagner's Theorem.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Sketch the **inductive proof** of the (⇒) direction of Wagner's Theorem on the number of vertices $n$. Clearly state the two cases and the role of Euler's formula in Case B.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Wagner's Theorem** with **Kuratowski's Theorem**. State the Robertson–Seymour Theorem (Wagner's Conjecture) and explain its significance.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Induction hypothesis:** Every non-planar graph on fewer than $n$ vertices contains $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ as a minor.

**Base cases:** $n \le 5$ (only non-planar candidate is $K_5$) and $n = 6$ (handled by induction after contraction of an attached vertex).

**Case A — $G$ has a vertex of degree $\le 3$:** Delete $v$ to get $G' = G - v$. $G'$ is non-planar (else $v$ could be reinserted into a face of the planar embedding, since $\le 3$ neighbours). By induction, $G'$ has a $K_5$ or $K_{3,3}$ minor; this is also a minor of $G$.

**Case B — Every vertex has degree $\ge 4$:** By Handshake, $E \ge 2n$. Assuming $G$ is planar, Euler's formula gives
$$F = 2 - n + E \ge 2 - n + 2n = n + 2.$$
A discharging argument (assign initial charge $\deg - 4$ to each face and vertex, total $-8$, redistribute via edges) shows a small face of length $\le 7$ must exist. Contracting an appropriate edge of this small face yields a smaller non-planar graph to which the induction hypothesis applies, lifting the minor back to $G$.

**Role of Euler's formula:** It provides the bound $F \ge n + 2$ that, combined with the average face-degree $2E / F$, guarantees a small face — the launching pad for the contraction step that reduces the problem to a smaller instance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Feature | Kuratowski's Theorem | Wagner's Theorem |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forbidden objects** | Subdivisions of $K_5$, $K_{3,3}$ | Minors $K_5$, $K_{3,3}$ |
| **Operations allowed** | Subdivision, deletion | Contraction, deletion |
| **Year / Author** | 1930, K. Kuratowski | 1937, K. Wagner |
| **Implication** | Subdivision $\Rightarrow$ Minor | Minor is the *weaker* condition |
| **Verification difficulty** | Usually easier in practice | Usually requires search algorithms |

**Robertson–Seymour Theorem (2004, proof of Wagner's Conjecture, 1980s–2004):**
> For every infinite family of finite graphs closed under the minor operation, there is a **finite** set of **forbidden minors** such that the family consists exactly of the graphs that avoid all of them.

**Significance:**
1. It generalizes Wagner's Theorem to *every* minor-closed family (e.g., forests, series-parallel graphs, outer-planar graphs, graphs of bounded tree-width, …).
2. It explains why every such family has a finite obstruction set — including the two-element set $\{K_5, K_{3,3}\}$ for the planar family.
3. It launched the modern theory of **graph structure theory** and is foundational for algorithmic graph minors (e.g., bidimensionality theory, algorithmic meta-theorems).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on Wagner's Theorem questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing minors with subgraphs.** A *subdivision* of $K_5$ inside $G$ does **not** automatically give a $K_5$ *minor*. The student must explicitly identify connected **branch sets** — not just a topological copy.
> 2. **Forgetting the bipartite edge bound.** When verifying $K_{3,3}$ is non-planar, many students apply $3V - 6$ instead of the tighter $2V - 4$ bipartite bound, losing a mark for incomplete rigor.
> 3. **Skipping the discharging argument.** In Case B of the (⇒) proof, examiners expect at least a *sketch* of the discharging calculation ($\sum (\deg - 4) = -8$). Writing only "by Euler's formula" is insufficient.
> 4. **Mixing up the two directions of the biconditional.** Always label "(⇐)" and "(⇒)" clearly. The easier direction is (⇐); spending time on it during the (⇒) part costs marks.
> 5. **Writing "Kuratowski" instead of "Wagner" or vice-versa.** The two theorems are *not* identical. Examiners deduct marks if you cite the wrong author or the wrong forbidden structure.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Wagner's Theorem:** $G$ planar $\iff K_5 \not\preceq_m G$ and $K_{3,3} \not\preceq_m G$.
- ✅ **Minor =** vertex deletion + edge deletion + edge contraction.
- ✅ **The two forbidden minors** are $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$.
- ✅ **Planarity is closed under minors** — contraction/deletion cannot introduce crossings.
- ✅ **$K_5$ is non-planar** by $E = 10 > 9 = 3V - 6$.
- ✅ **$K_{3,3}$ is non-planar** by $E = 9 > 8 = 2V - 4$ (bipartite bound).
- ✅ **Proof of (⇒) uses induction on $|V|$** with a degree-based case split.
- ✅ **Euler's formula** $V - E + F = 2$ is the engine of Case B (high-degree regime).
- ✅ **Discharging total charge = $-8$** is the standard discharging identity in planar proofs.
- ✅ **Wagner's Theorem is a special case** of the Robertson–Seymour Graph Minor Theorem.
- ✅ **Branch sets** must be vertex-disjoint and connected to certify a minor.
- ✅ **Kuratowski = subdivisions; Wagner = minors.** Both characterize planarity via the same two forbidden graphs.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** VLSI routing, PCB layout, network topology verification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Graphs and Edge-Coloring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCAT504 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Line Graphs and Edge-Coloring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Line Graph — Formal Academic Definition

Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected simple graph with vertex set $V$ and edge set $E$. The **Line Graph** of $G$, denoted $L(G) = (V', E')$, is a graph constructed as follows:

$$V(L(G)) = E(G)$$

That is, **every edge of $G$ becomes a vertex in $L(G)$**. Two distinct vertices $e_1$ and $e_2$ in $L(G)$ are joined by an edge if and only if the corresponding edges in $G$ share a common endpoint (i.e., they are **adjacent edges** in $G$).

$$\{e_1, e_2\} \in E(L(G)) \iff e_1 \cap e_2 \neq \emptyset \text{ in } G$$

Formally, $L(G) = (E(G), F)$ where $F = \{\{e_1, e_2\} \subseteq E(G) \mid e_1 \text{ and } e_2 \text{ share an endpoint in } G\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The line graph is a vertex-critical transformation that converts **edge-adjacency problems in $G$** into **vertex-adjacency problems in $L(G)$**. This duality is the central intellectual engine of this module.

## 1.2 Edge-Coloring — Formal Academic Definition

An **Edge-Coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is an assignment of colors (or labels) to the edges of $G$ such that **no two incident edges (edges sharing a common vertex) receive the same color**. 

A **proper edge-coloring** uses $k$ colors if it is a function:

$$c : E(G) \to \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, k\}$$

such that for every vertex $v \in V(G)$ and every pair of edges $e_1, e_2$ incident to $v$, we have $c(e_1) \neq c(e_2)$.

The **Chromatic Index** (or **Edge-Chromatic Number**) of $G$, denoted $\chi'(G)$, is the **minimum number of colors** required to obtain a proper edge-coloring of $G$.

$$\chi'(G) = \min\{k \mid G \text{ admits a proper edge-coloring with } k \text{ colors}\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Equivalence Theorem:** The problem of edge-coloring $G$ with $k$ colors is **mathematically equivalent** to the problem of vertex-coloring $L(G)$ with $k$ colors. Therefore:
> $$\chi'(G) = \chi(L(G))$$
> where $\chi(L(G))$ is the ordinary chromatic number of the line graph. This is the **core duality** you must memorize for KTU exams.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy for Line Graphs
Imagine a **city subway map** where the original graph $G$ represents **stations (vertices) and direct routes between them (edges)**. Now imagine creating a *second-level map* $L(G)$ where **each route becomes a station**, and two route-stations are connected if those routes share a stop. This second map is the line graph — it answers questions like *"Which routes intersect with which other routes?"* in a single-layer adjacency structure.

### Analogy for Edge-Coloring
Think of **edge-coloring as scheduling**. Suppose you are a university timetable coordinator. Each **course** is a vertex (instructor), each **class session** is an edge connecting instructor to classroom, and each **time slot** is a color. Two sessions cannot use the same instructor at the same time (incident edges = same instructor) — so you assign each session a unique time slot for that instructor. The **chromatic index is the minimum number of time slots** needed to schedule all sessions without conflict.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Edge-coloring G = Vertex-coloring L(G)."* Memorize this single sentence — it unlocks 80% of problems in this module.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
|:------:|:--------|:-----|
| $\Delta(G)$ | Maximum degree of any vertex in $G$ | Integer |
| $\delta(G)$ | Minimum degree of any vertex in $G$ | Integer |
| $\chi(G)$ | Vertex-chromatic number of $G$ | Integer |
| $\chi'(G)$ | Edge-chromatic number (chromatic index) of $G$ | Integer |
| $L(G)$ | Line graph of $G$ | Graph |
| $L^{(k)}(G)$ | $k$-th iterated line graph | Graph |
| $m$ | Number of edges in $G$ | Integer |
| $n$ | Number of vertices in $G$ | Integer |
| $q$ | Number of edges in $L(G)$ | Integer |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Properties of Line Graphs

### 2.1.1 Number of Vertices in $L(G)$

Since each edge of $G$ becomes a vertex in $L(G)$:

$$|V(L(G))| = |E(G)| = m$$

### 2.1.2 Number of Edges in $L(G)$

For each vertex $v \in V(G)$ with degree $d(v)$, the edges incident to $v$ in $G$ form a **clique** $K_{d(v)}$ in $L(G)$. The number of edges contributed by vertex $v$ is $\binom{d(v)}{2}$. Summing over all vertices:

$$|E(L(G))| = \sum_{v \in V(G)} \binom{d(v)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{v \in V(G)} d(v)\bigl(d(v) - 1\bigr)$$

Using the handshaking lemma $\sum_{v} d(v) = 2m$:

$$|E(L(G))| = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{v \in V(G)} d(v)^2 - m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **By Cauchy-Schwarz / Jensen's Inequality:** $\sum_{v} d(v)^2 \geq \dfrac{(2m)^2}{n} = \dfrac{4m^2}{n}$, hence $|E(L(G))| \geq \dfrac{2m^2}{n} - m$.

### 2.1.3 Degree of a Vertex in $L(G)$

If $e = \{u, v\}$ is an edge of $G$, then the degree of vertex $e$ in $L(G)$ equals:

$$d_{L(G)}(e) = d_G(u) + d_G(v) - 2$$

This is because edge $e$ is adjacent (in $L(G)$) to every other edge incident to $u$ (there are $d_G(u) - 1$ such edges) and to every other edge incident to $v$ (there are $d_G(v) - 1$ such edges).

### 2.1.4 Cliques in $L(G)$

For every vertex $v$ of $G$, the set of edges incident to $v$ forms a **clique of size $d(v)$** in $L(G)$. The maximum clique size in $L(G)$ equals $\Delta(G)$:

$$\omega(L(G)) = \Delta(G)$$

where $\omega$ denotes the clique number.

### 2.1.5 Other Important Properties

- **Connectivity:** $L(G)$ is connected if and only if $G$ is connected and $G$ is not a single edge.
- **Krausz Characterization:** A graph $H$ is a line graph if and only if its edges can be partitioned into **complete subgraphs** such that every vertex belongs to at most two of these complete subgraphs.
- **Forbidden Induced Subgraphs:** The graphs $L(G)$ exclude nine specific induced subgraphs (Krausz's theorem gives a polynomial-time recognition algorithm).

## 2.2 Fundamental Theorems for Edge-Coloring

### 2.2.1 Trivial Lower Bound

At every vertex $v$, the $d(v)$ incident edges must receive pairwise distinct colors. Therefore:

$$\chi'(G) \geq \Delta(G)$$

### 2.2.2 Vizing's Theorem (Class 1 / Class 2 Dichotomy) — 1964

For any simple undirected graph $G$:

$$\Delta(G) \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$$

- If $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$, then $G$ is called a **Class 1** graph.
- If $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G) + 1$, then $G$ is called a **Class 2** graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vizing's Theorem is non-constructive.** It does not tell you *which* class a graph belongs to — determining this is **NP-hard** in general. This is a common KTU question: *"State Vizing's theorem and discuss its complexity implications."*

### 2.2.3 König's Theorem (Bipartite Graphs) — 1916

For any bipartite graph $G$:

$$\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$$

That is, **every bipartite graph is Class 1**. The proof uses **maximal matching** augmentation and provides a polynomial-time algorithm via **alternating-path swapping** (the foundation of the Hungarian method and Edmonds' matching algorithm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** For multigraphs (graphs with parallel edges), the upper bound is $\chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + \mu$, where $\mu$ is the maximum edge multiplicity (Shannon's Theorem, 1949).

### 2.2.4 Shannon's Theorem — 1949

For any multigraph $G$ with maximum edge multiplicity $\mu$:

$$\chi'(G) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{3\Delta(G)}{2} \right\rfloor$$

## 2.3 Special Graph Results

| Graph $G$ | $\chi'(G)$ | Class |
|:----------|:-----------|:------|
| Path $P_n$ ($n \geq 2$) | $2$ if $n \geq 2$ | Class 1 |
| Cycle $C_n$ | $2$ if $n$ even, $3$ if $n$ odd | Class 1 / Class 2 |
| Complete $K_n$ | $n-1$ if $n$ even, $n$ if $n$ odd | Class 1 / Class 2 |
| Complete bipartite $K_{m,n}$ | $\max(m, n)$ | Class 1 |
| Tree $T$ with $\Delta(T) \geq 2$ | $\Delta(T)$ | Class 1 |
| Petersen graph | $4$ (where $\Delta = 3$) | **Class 2** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement |
|:-:|:------------------|:----------|
| 1 | $|V(L(G))|$ | $= m = \vert E(G)\vert$ |
| 2 | $|E(L(G))|$ | $= \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{v \in V} d(v)\bigl(d(v)-1\bigr)$ |
| 3 | $d_{L(G)}(e=\{u,v\})$ | $= d(u) + d(v) - 2$ |
| 4 | $\omega(L(G))$ | $= \Delta(G)$ |
| 5 | **Trivial Bound** | $\chi'(G) \geq \Delta(G)$ |
| 6 | **Vizing's Theorem** | $\Delta(G) \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ |
| 7 | **König's Theorem** | $G$ bipartite $\Rightarrow \chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$ |
| 8 | **Shannon's Theorem** | $\chi'(G) \leq \lfloor 3\Delta(G)/2 \rfloor$ |
| 9 | **Duality** | $\chi'(G) = \chi(L(G))$ |
| 10 | **Petersen (1891)** | $\chi'(K_n) = n-1$ if $n$ even, $n$ if $n$ odd |
| 11 | **Iterated** | $L^{(k+1)}(G) = L(L^{(k)}(G))$; $L^{(k)}(K_3) = K_3$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Edge-coloring models **frequency assignment in wireless networks** (T-1 carriers in satellite communication, channel assignment in cellular systems, register allocation in compilers). Every cell tower = vertex, every frequency = color, two towers within interference range = edge.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Construction of a Line Graph — Worked Example

Let us derive $L(G)$ for a sample graph and prove the degree formula.

**Given:** $G = K_4$ minus one edge. Specifically, $G$ has vertices $V = \{a, b, c, d\}$ and edges $E = \{ab, ac, ad, bc, bd\}$ (5 edges; $cd$ removed).

**Step 1:** Identify edge set $E(G) = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5\}$ where:
- $e_1 = ab$
- $e_2 = ac$
- $e_3 = ad$
- $e_4 = bc$
- $e_5 = bd$

**Step 2:** The vertex set of $L(G)$ is $V(L(G)) = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5\}$.

**Step 3:** Determine adjacencies. Two edges of $G$ are adjacent in $L(G)$ iff they share a vertex in $G$. List all pairs:

| Pair | Common Vertex? | Edge in $L(G)$? |
|:----:|:---------------|:----------------:|
| $\{e_1, e_2\} = \{ab, ac\}$ | $a$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_1, e_3\} = \{ab, ad\}$ | $a$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_1, e_4\} = \{ab, bc\}$ | $b$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_1, e_5\} = \{ab, bd\}$ | $b$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_2, e_3\} = \{ac, ad\}$ | $a$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_2, e_4\} = \{ac, bc\}$ | $c$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_2, e_5\} = \{ac, bd\}$ | none | **No** |
| $\{e_3, e_4\} = \{ad, bc\}$ | none | **No** |
| $\{e_3, e_5\} = \{ad, bd\}$ | $d$ | **Yes** |
| $\{e_4, e_5\} = \{bc, bd\}$ | $b$ | **Yes** |

**Step 4:** Verify the degree formula. Take $e_1 = ab$. Endpoint degrees: $d(a) = 3$ (edges $ab, ac, ad$), $d(b) = 3$ (edges $ab, bc, bd$). By the formula:

$$d_{L(G)}(e_1) = d(a) + d(b) - 2 = 3 + 3 - 2 = 4$$

Direct count from Step 3: $e_1$ is adjacent to $\{e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5\}$ — but wait, $e_5$ is adjacent, so we have $e_2, e_3, e_4, e_5$ giving degree 4. **Verified.** ✓

**Step 5:** Compute total edge count of $L(G)$:

$$|E(L(G))| = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{v \in V} d(v)\bigl(d(v) - 1\bigr) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[3\cdot 2 + 3\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1\bigr] = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[6 + 6 + 2 + 2\bigr] = 8$$

From the adjacency table, count "Yes" entries: 8 pairs. **Verified.** ✓

## 3.2 Proof of Vizing's Theorem (Sketch with Full Justification)

**Theorem:** For any simple graph $G$, $\chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$.

**Proof Outline (Proof by Construction via "Fan Argument"):**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Suppose we have a proper edge-coloring of $G$ using only colors from $\{1, 2, \ldots, \Delta(G)\}$ that is *almost* complete — all edges are colored except for a single uncolored edge $e = \{u, v\}$. We will show we can always extend the coloring.

**Step 2 — Kempe Chain Definition:** For a color $i$, the subgraph $G_i \subseteq G$ consists of all edges colored $i$. This is a **matching** (since adjacent edges cannot share color). A **Kempe chain** (or **alternating path**) at vertex $u$ with respect to colors $i, j$ is the maximal connected component of $G_i \cup G_j$ that contains $u$.

**Step 3 — Fan Construction:** Consider the uncolored edge $e = \{u, v\}$. At vertex $u$, there are $d(u) \leq \Delta(G)$ incident edges, each colored with some color. List the neighbors of $u$ in some order: $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{d(u)}$. The edges $\{u, v_k\}$ are colored $c_k$. Build a "fan" — a sequence of edges at $u$.

**Step 4 — Finding a Free Color:** Either there exists a color $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, \Delta(G)+1\}$ not used at $u$ (then color $e$ with $j$ and finish), OR all $\Delta(G) + 1$ colors are used. But $u$ has only $d(u) \leq \Delta(G)$ incident edges, so at most $\Delta(G)$ colors appear at $u$. Therefore **at least one color** (call it $\alpha$) is free at $u$, and **at least one color** (call it $\beta$) is free at $v$.

**Step 5 — Swap Argument:** Try to recolor the fan by swapping colors $\alpha$ and $\beta$ along Kempe chains. If a Kempe chain from $u$ in colors $\{\alpha, \beta\}$ does not reach $v$, swap colors along it, freeing $\beta$ at $u$ and allowing us to color $e$. If it does reach $v$, a more sophisticated fan-shifting argument rotates the fan and either frees a color at $u$ or creates a "missing" color that can be propagated.

**Step 6 — Termination:** The fan has finite length, and each recoloring strictly reduces the lexicographic measure. Thus the process terminates with a proper $(\Delta+1)$-edge-coloring. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The full proof is rarely asked, but you must be able to reproduce the *structure*: the fan argument + Kempe chain + termination via lexicographic measure. Marks are awarded for each conceptual step.

## 3.3 Proof Sketch of König's Theorem (Bipartite Edge-Coloring)

**Theorem:** If $G$ is bipartite, then $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$.

**Proof by Induction on Number of Edges:**

**Base Case:** $G$ has no edges → $\chi'(G) = 0 = \Delta(G)$. ✓

**Inductive Step:** Assume true for all bipartite graphs with fewer than $m$ edges. Let $G$ be bipartite with $m$ edges.

**Step A (Existence of Perfect-ish Matching):** By Hall's Marriage Theorem applied to a balanced bipartite structure, or via König's matching theorem directly, $G$ contains a **matching that saturates all maximum-degree vertices**. More precisely: let $M$ be a **maximum matching** in $G$. The number of unmatched vertices is $|V| - 2|M|$.

**Step B (Key Lemma):** *In a bipartite graph, the size of a maximum matching equals the size of a minimum vertex cover* (König's theorem on matchings). Therefore we can find a vertex cover $C$ with $|C| = |M|$.

**Step C (Greedy Augmentation):** Color all edges in $M$ with **color 1**. Remove $M$ from $G$. The remaining graph $G' = G - M$ is still bipartite and has strictly fewer edges. By induction, $G'$ admits a proper edge-coloring with $\Delta(G') \leq \Delta(G)$ colors.

**Step D (Degree Bound):** Each vertex loses at most one incident edge (the one in $M$). Therefore $\Delta(G') \leq \Delta(G) - 1 < \Delta(G)$.

**Step E (Conclusion):** Using $\Delta(G) - 1 + 1 = \Delta(G)$ colors total, we obtain a proper edge-coloring of $G$. Hence $\chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G)$. Combined with the trivial lower bound $\chi'(G) \geq \Delta(G)$, we get $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Algorithmic Construction: Building $L(G)$

For algorithmic purposes (e.g., in a KTU lab/programming exam):

**Algorithm:** Construct-Line-Graph($G = (V, E)$)

**Input:** Adjacency lists $Adj[v]$ for each $v \in V(G)$.

**Output:** Adjacency lists $L\_Adj[e]$ for each $e \in E(L(G))$.

```
1.  Initialize L_Adj as empty dictionary
2.  For each edge e = {u, v} in E(G):
3.      L_Adj[e] ← empty list
4.  For each vertex v in V(G):
5.      Let E_v = list of edges incident to v
6.      For each unordered pair (e_i, e_j) in E_v:
7.          Append e_j to L_Adj[e_i]
8.          Append e_i to L_Adj[e_j]
9.  Return L_Adj
```

**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m + \sum_{v} d(v)^2) = O(n + m + \sum_{v} d(v)^2)$. In dense graphs, this is $O(n^3)$; in sparse graphs, $O(m \cdot \Delta(G))$.

## 3.5 Edge-Coloring Algorithm (Bipartite Case — Based on König's Proof)

**Algorithm:** Edge-Color-Bipartite($G = (X \cup Y, E)$)

```
1.  Compute Δ = max degree
2.  For k = 1 to Δ:
3.      Find a maximum matching M_k in G (Hungarian/Edmonds)
4.      Color all edges in M_k with color k
5.      G ← G - M_k
6.  Return coloring c : E → {1, 2, ..., Δ}
```

**Time Complexity:** Using the Hopcroft–Karp algorithm, each matching takes $O(\sqrt{n} \cdot m)$, and we run it $\Delta$ times, yielding total $O(\Delta \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot m)$.

## 3.6 Iterated Line Graphs — The $L^{(k)}(K_3)$ Case

A famous result states that for the complete graph $K_3$ (a triangle):

$$L(K_3) = K_3$$

since each edge of the triangle shares a vertex with the other two edges, forming a triangle. Therefore $L^{(k)}(K_3) = K_3$ for all $k \geq 1$. This is a **fixed point** of the line graph operator.

For $K_4$: $L(K_4) = K_6$ minus a perfect matching (the cocktail party graph $K_{2,2,2}$). For $K_n$ in general, $L(K_n)$ is strongly regular with parameters depending on $n$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Original Graph $G$ (Left) and Its Line Graph $L(G)$ (Right)

The following schematic shows the **transformation process** from $G$ to $L(G)$ using a sample 4-vertex graph with 5 edges. Each edge in $G$ (left) becomes a labeled node in $L(G)$ (right), with edges in $L(G)$ drawn between incident edges in $G$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph G_Original["Original Graph G"]
        vA["Vertex A"] --- e1["e1 = AB"]
        vA --- e2["e2 = AC"]
        vA --- e3["e3 = AD"]
        vB["Vertex B"] --- e1
        vB --- e4["e4 = BC"]
        vB --- e5["e5 = BD"]
        vC["Vertex C"] --- e2
        vC --- e4
        vD["Vertex D"] --- e3
        vD --- e5
    end

    subgraph LG_Result["Line Graph L of G"]
        n1["e1"] --- n2["e2"]
        n1 --- n3["e3"]
        n1 --- n4["e4"]
        n1 --- n5["e5"]
        n2 --- n3
        n2 --- n4
        n3 --- n5
        n4 --- n5
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each edge in $G$ corresponds to a single node in $L(G)$. Two nodes in $L(G)$ are connected whenever the corresponding edges in $G$ share a vertex. For instance, $e_1 = AB$ and $e_2 = AC$ share vertex $A$, so $e_1$ and $e_2$ are adjacent in $L(G)$.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Edge-Coloring Pipeline

The following diagram outlines the **end-to-end edge-coloring workflow** as a functional block-architecture. It shows the data flow from input graph → line graph construction → vertex-coloring → reverse mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G = V,E] --> B[Extract Edge Set E]
    B --> C[For each edge e in E, create vertex ve in L of G]
    C --> D[For each pair of edges in G sharing a vertex, add edge in L of G]
    D --> E[Line Graph L of G ready]
    E --> F[Apply Vertex Coloring Algorithm to L of G]
    F --> G[Use DSATUR or Welsh-Powell]
    G --> H[Assign k = chi of L of G colors to vertices of L of G]
    H --> I[Map colors back: each vertex in L of G corresponds to an edge in G]
    I --> J[Output Edge Coloring c : E rightarrow 1 to k]
    J --> K[Verify: no two incident edges share a color]
    K --> L{Valid?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Return chi prime of G = k]
    L -- No --> N[Increment k and re-run from step G]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Class 1 vs Class 2 Classification

A flowchart to **classify a graph as Class 1 or Class 2** based on its structural properties.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Given Graph G] --> Q1{Is G bipartite?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> C1[Class 1 by Konig: chi prime of G = Delta]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is G a complete graph Kn?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{n is even?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> C2[Class 1: chi prime = n minus 1 = Delta]
    Q3 -- No --> C3[Class 2: chi prime = n = Delta + 1]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Apply Vizing Bound}
    Q4 --> Q5{Try greedy coloring with Delta colors}
    Q5 -- Success --> C4[Class 1]
    Q5 -- Failure --> C5[Class 2 by Vizing: chi prime = Delta + 1]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Table: Vertex-Coloring vs Edge-Coloring

| Aspect | Vertex-Coloring $\chi(G)$ | Edge-Coloring $\chi'(G)$ |
|:-------|:--------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Object colored | Vertices | Edges |
| Constraint | Adjacent vertices different colors | Incident edges different colors |
| Lower bound | $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ | $\chi'(G) \geq \Delta(G)$ |
| Bipartite special | $\chi(G) = 2$ | $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$ |
| 4-Color Theorem | $\chi(\text{planar}) \leq 4$ | No direct analog |
| Duality identity | — | $\chi'(G) = \chi(L(G))$ |
| NP-hard? | Yes (in general) | Yes (in general) |
| Polynomial for special cases | Bipartite, interval, chordal | Bipartite (König) |
